**Montreal**[[note 1]](#cite_note-13) ([French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *Montréal*)[[note 2]](#cite_note-15) is the [largest city](/wiki/List_of_towns_in_Quebec "List of towns in Quebec") in the [province](/wiki/Provinces_and_territories_of_Canada "Provinces and territories of Canada") of [Quebec](/wiki/Quebec "Quebec"), the [second-largest](/wiki/List_of_the_largest_municipalities_in_Canada_by_population "List of the largest municipalities in Canada by population") in Canada, and the [eighth-largest city in North America](/wiki/List_of_North_American_cities_by_population "List of North American cities by population"). Founded in 1642 as *[Ville-Marie](/wiki/Fort_Ville-Marie "Fort Ville-Marie")*, or "City of Mary",[[14]](#cite_note-vieux.montreal.qc.ca-16) it now takes its name from [Mount Royal](/wiki/Mount_Royal "Mount Royal"),[[15]](#cite_note-montreal.about.com-17) the triple-peaked mountain around which the early settlement was built.[[16]](#cite_note-18) The city is centred on the [Island of Montreal](/wiki/Island_of_Montreal "Island of Montreal")[[17]](#cite_note-19)[[18]](#cite_note-20) and a few, much smaller, peripheral islands, the largest of which is [Île Bizard](/wiki/%C3%8Ele_Bizard "Île Bizard"). It lies 196 kilometres (122 mi) east of the national capital, [Ottawa](/wiki/Ottawa "Ottawa"), and 258 kilometres (160 mi) southwest of the provincial capital, [Quebec City](/wiki/Quebec_City "Quebec City").

As of the [2021 Canadian census](/wiki/2021_Canadian_census "2021 Canadian census")[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Montreal&action=edit), the city had a population of 1,762,949 and a [metropolitan](/wiki/Census_geographic_units_of_Canada#Census_metropolitan_areas "Census geographic units of Canada") population of 4,291,732,[[7]](#cite_note-pop_city_cma_pc-7) making it the [second-largest metropolitan area](/wiki/List_of_census_metropolitan_areas_and_agglomerations_in_Canada "List of census metropolitan areas and agglomerations in Canada") in Canada after [Toronto, Ontario](/wiki/Toronto,_Ontario "Toronto, Ontario"). French is the city's official language.[[19]](#cite_note-21)[[20]](#cite_note-22) In 2021, 85.7% of the population of the city of Montreal considered themselves fluent in French while 90.2% could speak it in the metropolitan area.[[21]](#cite_note-23)[[22]](#cite_note-24) Montreal is one of the most [bilingual](/wiki/Bilingualism "Bilingualism") cities in Quebec and Canada, with 58.5% of the population able to speak both French and English.[[23]](#cite_note-cp2016-CD-25) Montreal is the largest primarily French speaking city in the Americas.

Historically the commercial capital of Canada, Montreal was surpassed in population and economic strength by Toronto in the 1970s.[[24]](#cite_note-26) It remains an important centre of art, [culture](/wiki/Culture_of_Montreal "Culture of Montreal"), literature, [film](/wiki/List_of_films_shot_in_Montreal "List of films shot in Montreal") and television, music, commerce, aerospace, [transport](/wiki/Transportation_in_Montreal "Transportation in Montreal"), finance, pharmaceuticals, technology, design, [education](/wiki/Education_in_Montreal "Education in Montreal"), [tourism](/wiki/Tourism_in_Montreal "Tourism in Montreal"), food, fashion, video game development, and world affairs. Montreal is the location of the headquarters of the [International Civil Aviation Organization](/wiki/International_Civil_Aviation_Organization "International Civil Aviation Organization"), and was named a [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") [City of Design](/wiki/Design_Cities_(UNESCO) "Design Cities (UNESCO)") in 2006.[[25]](#cite_note-UNESCO-27)[[26]](#cite_note-Monocle-28) In 2017, Montreal was ranked the 12th-most livable city in the world by the [Economist Intelligence Unit](/wiki/Economist_Intelligence_Unit "Economist Intelligence Unit") in its annual [Global Liveability Ranking](/wiki/Global_Liveability_Ranking "Global Liveability Ranking"),[[27]](#cite_note-29) although its ranking dropped to 40th in the 2021 index, primarily due to stress on the healthcare system caused by the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic").[[28]](#cite_note-30) It is regularly ranked as one of the ten best cities in the world to be a university student in the [QS World University Rankings](/wiki/QS_World_University_Rankings "QS World University Rankings").[[29]](#cite_note-test-31) In 2018, Montreal was ranked as a [global city](/wiki/Globalization_and_World_Cities_Research_Network "Globalization and World Cities Research Network").[[30]](#cite_note-32)

Montreal has hosted numerous important international events, including the [1967 International and Universal Exposition](/wiki/Expo_67 "Expo 67"), and is the only Canadian city to have hosted the Summer Olympics, having done so [in 1976](/wiki/1976_Summer_Olympics "1976 Summer Olympics").[[31]](#cite_note-33)[[32]](#cite_note-34) The city hosts the [Canadian Grand Prix](/wiki/Canadian_Grand_Prix "Canadian Grand Prix") of [Formula One](/wiki/Formula_One "Formula One");[[33]](#cite_note-35) the [Montreal International Jazz Festival](/wiki/Montreal_International_Jazz_Festival "Montreal International Jazz Festival"),[[34]](#cite_note-36) the largest jazz festival in the world;[[35]](#cite_note-37) the [Just for Laughs](/wiki/Just_for_Laughs "Just for Laughs") festival, the largest comedy festival in the world;[[36]](#cite_note-38) and [Les Francos de Montréal](/wiki/Les_Francos_de_Montr%C3%A9al "Les Francos de Montréal"), the largest French-language music festival in the world.[[37]](#cite_note-39) In [sports](/wiki/Sports_in_Montreal "Sports in Montreal"), it is home to multiple professional teams, most notably the [Canadiens](/wiki/Montreal_Canadiens "Montreal Canadiens") of the [National Hockey League](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League"), who have won the [Stanley Cup](/wiki/Stanley_Cup "Stanley Cup") a record 24 times.

## Etymology and original names

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Montreal&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology and original names")]

See also: [Name of Montreal](/wiki/Name_of_Montreal "Name of Montreal")

In the [Ojibwe language](/wiki/Ojibwe_language "Ojibwe language"), the land is called *Mooniyaang*[[38]](#cite_note-40) or *Moon’yaang*[[39]](#cite_note-41) which was "the first stopping place" in the Ojibwe migration story as related in the [seven fires prophecy](/wiki/Seven_fires_prophecy "Seven fires prophecy").

In the [Mohawk language](/wiki/Mohawk_language "Mohawk language"), the land is called *Tiohtià:ke*.[[40]](#cite_note-42)[[41]](#cite_note-43)[[42]](#cite_note-concordia-land-44)[[43]](#cite_note-45) This is an abbreviation of *Teionihtiohtiá:kon*, which loosely translates as "where the group divided/parted ways".[[42]](#cite_note-concordia-land-44)[[44]](#cite_note-46)

French settlers from [La Flèche](/wiki/La_Fl%C3%A8che "La Flèche") in the Loire Valley first named their new town, founded in 1642, *[Ville Marie](/wiki/Fort_Ville-Marie "Fort Ville-Marie")* ("City of Mary"),[[14]](#cite_note-vieux.montreal.qc.ca-16) named for the [Virgin Mary](/wiki/Virgin_Mary "Virgin Mary").[[45]](#cite_note-47)

The current form of the name, *Montréal*, is generally thought to be derived from [Mount Royal](/wiki/Mount_Royal "Mount Royal") (*Mont Royal* in French),[[15]](#cite_note-montreal.about.com-17)[[46]](#cite_note-:0-48) the triple-peaked hill in the heart of the city. There are multiple explanations for how *Mont Royal* became *Montréal*. In sixteenth-century French, the forms *réal* and *royal* were used interchangeably, so *Montréal* could simply be a variant of *Mont Royal*.[[47]](#cite_note-49)[[48]](#cite_note-montrealgazette.com-50)[[49]](#cite_note-51) In the second explanation, the name came from an Italian translation. Venetian geographer [Giovanni Battista Ramusio](/wiki/Giovanni_Battista_Ramusio "Giovanni Battista Ramusio") used the name *Monte Real* to designate Mount Royal in his 1556 map of the region.[[46]](#cite_note-:0-48) However, this explanation is disputed by the [Commission de toponymie du Québec](/wiki/Commission_de_toponymie_du_Qu%C3%A9bec "Commission de toponymie du Québec").[[48]](#cite_note-montrealgazette.com-50)

In English, the accent mark is often omitted (*Montreal*); however, officially, the name includes the accent mark (*Montréal*),[[50]](#cite_note-52)[[51]](#cite_note-53)[[a]](#cite_note-55) even though [English rarely uses diacritical marks](/wiki/English_terms_with_diacritical_marks "English terms with diacritical marks").[[53]](#cite_note-56)

[François de Belleforest](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_de_Belleforest "François de Belleforest") was the first to use the form *Montréal* with reference to the entire region in 1575.[[46]](#cite_note-:0-48)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Montreal&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of Montreal](/wiki/History_of_Montreal "History of Montreal")

For a chronological guide, see [Timeline of Montreal history](/wiki/Timeline_of_Montreal_history "Timeline of Montreal history").

### Pre-European contact

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Montreal&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Pre-European contact")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/25/Jacques_Cartier_a_Hochelaga.jpg/250px-Jacques_Cartier_a_Hochelaga.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jacques_Cartier_a_Hochelaga.jpg)

[Jacques Cartier](/wiki/Jacques_Cartier "Jacques Cartier") at [Hochelaga](/wiki/Hochelaga_(village) "Hochelaga (village)"). Arriving in 1535, Cartier was the first European to visit the area.

[Archaeological](/wiki/Archaeological "Archaeological") evidence in the region indicates that [First Nations](/wiki/First_Nations_in_Canada "First Nations in Canada") native people occupied the island of Montreal as early as 4,000 years ago.[[54]](#cite_note-57) By the year AD 1000, they had started to cultivate [maize](/wiki/Maize "Maize"). Within a few hundred years, they had built [fortified](/wiki/Fortified "Fortified") villages.[[55]](#cite_note-58) The [Saint Lawrence Iroquoians](/wiki/Saint_Lawrence_Iroquoians "Saint Lawrence Iroquoians"), an ethnically and culturally distinct group from the [Iroquois](/wiki/Iroquois "Iroquois") nations of the *[Haudenosaunee](/wiki/Haudenosaunee "Haudenosaunee")* (then based in present-day New York), established the [village of Hochelaga](/wiki/Hochelaga_(village) "Hochelaga (village)") at the foot of Mount Royal two centuries before the French arrived. Archeologists have found evidence of their habitation there and at other locations in the valley since at least the 14th century.[[56]](#cite_note-Tremblay-59) The French explorer Jacques Cartier visited *Hochelaga* on October 2, 1535, and estimated the population of the native people at Hochelaga to be "over a thousand people".[[56]](#cite_note-Tremblay-59) Evidence of earlier occupation of the island, such as those uncovered in 1642 during the construction of Fort Ville-Marie, have effectively been removed.

### Early European settlement (1600–1760)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Montreal&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Early European settlement (1600–1760)")]

In 1603, French explorer [Samuel de Champlain](/wiki/Samuel_de_Champlain "Samuel de Champlain") reported that the St Lawrence Iroquoians and their settlements had disappeared altogether from the St Lawrence valley. This is believed to be due to outmigration, epidemics of European diseases, or intertribal wars.[[56]](#cite_note-Tremblay-59)[[57]](#cite_note-60) In 1611, Champlain established a [fur](/wiki/Fur "Fur") [trading post](/wiki/Trading_post "Trading post") on the Island of Montreal on a site initially named *La Place Royale*. At the confluence of *Petite Riviere* and [St. Lawrence River](/wiki/St._Lawrence_River "St. Lawrence River"), it is where present-day [Pointe-à-Callière](/wiki/Pointe-%C3%A0-Calli%C3%A8re "Pointe-à-Callière") stands.[[58]](#cite_note-Marsan-61) On his 1616 map, Champlain named the island Lille de Villemenon in honour of the sieur de Villemenon, a French dignitary who was seeking the viceroyship of New France.[[59]](#cite_note-62) In 1639, [Jérôme Le Royer de La Dauversière](/wiki/J%C3%A9r%C3%B4me_Le_Royer_de_La_Dauversi%C3%A8re "Jérôme Le Royer de La Dauversière") obtained the [Seigneurial title](/wiki/Seigneurial_system_of_New_France "Seigneurial system of New France") to the Island of Montreal in the name of the [Notre Dame Society of Montreal](/wiki/Soci%C3%A9t%C3%A9_de_Notre-Dame_de_Montr%C3%A9al "Société de Notre-Dame de Montréal") to establish a Roman Catholic [mission](/wiki/Mission_(Christian) "Mission (Christian)") to [evangelize](/wiki/Evangelize "Evangelize") natives.

[Dauversière](/wiki/J%C3%A9r%C3%B4me_le_Royer_de_la_Dauversi%C3%A8re "Jérôme le Royer de la Dauversière") hired [Paul Chomedey de Maisonneuve](/wiki/Paul_Chomedey_de_Maisonneuve "Paul Chomedey de Maisonneuve"), then age 30, to lead a group of colonists to build a mission on his new seigneury. The colonists left France in 1641 for Quebec and arrived on the island the following year. On May 17, 1642, Ville-Marie was founded on the southern shore of Montreal island, with Maisonneuve as its first governor. The settlement included a chapel and a hospital, under the command of [Jeanne Mance](/wiki/Jeanne_Mance "Jeanne Mance").[[60]](#cite_note-Miquelon-63) By 1643, Ville-Marie had come under Iroquois raids. In 1652, Maisonneuve returned to France to raise 100 volunteers to bolster the colonial population. If the effort had failed, Montreal was to be abandoned and the survivors re-located downriver to [Quebec City](/wiki/Quebec_City "Quebec City"). Before these 100 arrived in the fall of 1653, the population of Montreal was barely 50 people.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Capitulation_Montreal.jpg/250px-Capitulation_Montreal.jpg)](/wiki/File:Capitulation_Montreal.jpg)

French authorities surrender the city of Montreal to the British after the [Articles of Capitulation](/wiki/Articles_of_Capitulation_of_Montreal "Articles of Capitulation of Montreal") was signed in 1760.

By 1685, Ville-Marie was home to some 600 colonists, most of them living in modest wooden houses. Ville-Marie became a centre for the [fur trade](/wiki/Fur_trade "Fur trade") and a base for further [exploration](/wiki/French_colonization_of_the_Americas "French colonization of the Americas").[[60]](#cite_note-Miquelon-63) In 1689, the English-allied Iroquois [attacked Lachine](/wiki/Lachine_massacre "Lachine massacre") on the Island of Montreal, committing the worst massacre in the history of New France.[[61]](#cite_note-64) By the early 18th century, the [Sulpician Order](/wiki/Sulpician_Order "Sulpician Order") was established there. To encourage French settlement, it wanted the Mohawk to move away from the fur trading post at Ville-Marie. It had a mission village, known as [Kahnewake](/wiki/Kahnewake "Kahnewake"), south of the St Lawrence River. The fathers persuaded some Mohawk to make a new settlement at their former hunting grounds north of the Ottawa River. This became [Kanesatake](/wiki/Kanesatake "Kanesatake").[[62]](#cite_note-65) In 1745, several Mohawk families moved upriver to create another settlement, known as [Akwesasne](/wiki/Akwesasne "Akwesasne"). All three are now Mohawk reserves in Canada. The Canadian territory was ruled as a French colony until 1760, when [Montreal fell to a British offensive](/wiki/Montreal_Campaign "Montreal Campaign") during the [Seven Years' War](/wiki/Seven_Years%27_War "Seven Years' War"). The colony then surrendered to Great Britain.[[63]](#cite_note-Encarta-66)

Ville-Marie was the name for the settlement that appeared in all official documents until 1705, when Montreal appeared for the first time, although people referred to the "Island of Montreal" long before then.[[64]](#cite_note-67)

### American occupation (1775–1776)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Montreal&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: American occupation (1775–1776)")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This section **does not [cite](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources") any [sources](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this section](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Montreal "Special:EditPage/Montreal") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and [removed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Burden_of_evidence "Wikipedia:Verifiability"). *(May 2022)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

As part of the [American Revolution](/wiki/American_Revolution "American Revolution"), the [invasion of Quebec](/wiki/Invasion_of_Quebec_(1775) "Invasion of Quebec (1775)") resulted after [Benedict Arnold](/wiki/Benedict_Arnold "Benedict Arnold") captured [Fort Ticonderoga](/wiki/Fort_Ticonderoga "Fort Ticonderoga") in present-day upstate New York in May 1775 as a launching point to [Arnold's invasion of Quebec in September](/wiki/Benedict_Arnold%27s_expedition_to_Quebec "Benedict Arnold's expedition to Quebec"). While Arnold approached the [Plains of Abraham](/wiki/Plains_of_Abraham "Plains of Abraham"), Montreal fell to American forces led by [Richard Montgomery](/wiki/Richard_Montgomery "Richard Montgomery") on November 13, 1775, after it was abandoned by [Guy Carleton](/wiki/Guy_Carleton,_1st_Baron_Dorchester "Guy Carleton, 1st Baron Dorchester"). After Arnold withdrew from Quebec City to [Pointe-aux-Trembles](/wiki/Pointe-aux-Trembles "Pointe-aux-Trembles") on November 19, Montgomery's forces left Montreal on December 1 and arrived there on December 3 to plot to [attack Quebec City](/wiki/Battle_of_Quebec_(1775) "Battle of Quebec (1775)"), with Montgomery leaving [David Wooster](/wiki/David_Wooster "David Wooster") in charge of the city. Montgomery was killed in the failed attack and Arnold, who had taken command, sent Brigadier General [Moses Hazen](/wiki/Moses_Hazen "Moses Hazen") to inform Wooster of the defeat.

Wooster left Hazen in command on March 20, 1776, as he left to replace Arnold in leading further attacks on Quebec City. On April 19, Arnold arrived in Montreal to take over command from Hazen, who remained as his second-in-command. Hazen sent Colonel [Timothy Bedel](/wiki/Timothy_Bedel "Timothy Bedel") to form a garrison of 390 men 40 miles upriver in a garrison at [Les Cèdres, Quebec](/wiki/Les_C%C3%A8dres,_Quebec "Les Cèdres, Quebec"), to defend Montreal against the British army. In the [Battle of the Cedars](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Cedars "Battle of the Cedars"), Bedel's lieutenant [Isaac Butterfield](/wiki/Isaac_Butterfield "Isaac Butterfield") surrendered to George Forster.

Forster advanced to [Fort Senneville](/wiki/Fort_Senneville "Fort Senneville") on May 23. By May 24, Arnold was entrenched in [Montreal's borough of Lachine](/wiki/Lachine,_Quebec "Lachine, Quebec"). Forster initially approached Lachine, then withdrew to Quinze-Chênes. Arnold's forces then abandoned Lachine to chase Forster. The Americans burned Senneville on May 26. After Arnold crossed the [Ottawa River](/wiki/Ottawa_River "Ottawa River") in pursuit of Forster, Forster's cannons repelled Arnold's forces. Forster negotiated a prisoner exchange with [Henry Sherburne](/wiki/Henry_Sherburne "Henry Sherburne") and Isaac Butterfield, resulting in a May 27 boating of their deputy Lieutenant Park being returned to the Americans. Arnold and Forster negotiated further and more American prisoners were returned to Arnold at [Sainte-Anne-de-Bellevue, Quebec](/wiki/Sainte-Anne-de-Bellevue,_Quebec "Sainte-Anne-de-Bellevue, Quebec"), ("Fort Anne") on May 30 (delayed two days by wind).

Arnold eventually withdrew his forces back to the New York fort of Ticonderoga by the summer. On June 15, Arnold's messenger approaching [Sorel](/wiki/Sorel-Tracy "Sorel-Tracy") spotted Carleton returning with a fleet of ships and notified him. Arnold's forces abandoned Montreal (attempting to burn it down in the process) prior to the June 17 arrival of Carleton's fleet.

The Americans did not return British prisoners in exchange, as previously agreed, due to accusations of abuse, with Congress repudiating the agreement at the protest of George Washington. Arnold blamed Colonel Timothy Bedel for the defeat, removing him and Lieutenant Butterfield from command and sending them to Sorel for court-martial. The retreat of the American army delayed their court martial until August 1, 1776, when they were convicted and [cashiered](/wiki/Cashiered "Cashiered") at Ticonderoga. Bedel was given a new commission by Congress in October 1777 after Arnold was assigned to defend Rhode Island in [July 1777](/wiki/Military_career_of_Benedict_Arnold,_1777%E2%80%931779 "Military career of Benedict Arnold, 1777–1779").

### Modern history as city (1832–present)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Montreal&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Modern history as city (1832–present)")]

See also: [20th-century municipal history of Quebec](/wiki/20th-century_municipal_history_of_Quebec "20th-century municipal history of Quebec")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b5/Lachine_Canal%2C_Montreal%2C_1826.jpg/250px-Lachine_Canal%2C_Montreal%2C_1826.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lachine_Canal,_Montreal,_1826.jpg)

View of [Lachine Canal](/wiki/Lachine_Canal "Lachine Canal") in 1826, a year after it opened. It bypassed the rapids west of the city, linking Montreal with other continental markets.

Montreal was incorporated as a city in 1832.[[65]](#cite_note-68) The opening of the Lachine Canal permitted ships to bypass the unnavigable [Lachine Rapids](/wiki/Lachine_Rapids "Lachine Rapids"),[[66]](#cite_note-Parks_Canada-69) while the construction of the [Victoria Bridge](/wiki/Victoria_Bridge_(Montreal) "Victoria Bridge (Montreal)") established Montreal as a major railway hub. The leaders of Montreal's business community started to build their homes in the [Golden Square Mile](/wiki/Golden_Square_Mile "Golden Square Mile") from about 1850. By 1860, it was the largest city under British rule in North America, culturally and economically dominating the rest of Canada.[[67]](#cite_note-UNAC-70)

In the 19th century, maintaining Montreal's drinking water became increasingly difficult with the rapid increase in population. A majority of the drinking water was still coming from the city's harbour, which was busy and heavily trafficked, leading to the deterioration of the water within. In the mid-1840s, the City of Montreal installed a water system that would pump water from the St. Lawrence and into [cisterns](/wiki/Cistern "Cistern"). The cisterns would then be transported to the desired location. This was not the first water system of its type in Montreal, as there had been one in private ownership since 1801. In the middle of the 19th century, water distribution was carried out by "fontainiers". The fountainiers[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] would open and close water valves outside of buildings, as directed, all over the city. As they lacked modern plumbing systems it was impossible to connect all buildings at once and it also acted as a conservation method. However, the population was not finished rising — it rose from 58,000 in 1852 to 267,000 by 1901.[[68]](#cite_note-71)[[69]](#cite_note-72)[[70]](#cite_note-73)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7c/Incendie_Parlement_Montreal.jpg/250px-Incendie_Parlement_Montreal.jpg)](/wiki/File:Incendie_Parlement_Montreal.jpg)

Political protests from [Tories](/wiki/Tories "Tories") led to the [burning of the Parliament Buildings in Montreal](/wiki/Burning_of_the_Parliament_Buildings_in_Montreal "Burning of the Parliament Buildings in Montreal") in 1849.

Montreal was the capital of the [Province of Canada](/wiki/Province_of_Canada "Province of Canada") from 1844 to 1849, but lost its status when a [Tory](/wiki/Toryism#Canada "Toryism") mob [burnt down the Parliament building](/wiki/Burning_of_the_Parliament_Buildings_in_Montreal "Burning of the Parliament Buildings in Montreal") to protest the passage of the [Rebellion Losses Bill](/wiki/Rebellion_Losses_Bill "Rebellion Losses Bill").[[71]](#cite_note-74) Thereafter, the capital rotated between Quebec City and [Toronto](/wiki/Toronto "Toronto") until in 1857, [Queen Victoria](/wiki/Queen_Victoria "Queen Victoria") herself established [Ottawa](/wiki/Ottawa "Ottawa") as the capital due to strategic reasons. The reasons were twofold. First, because it was located more in the interior of the Province of Canada, it was less susceptible to attack from the United States. Second, and perhaps more importantly, because it lay on the border between French and English Canada, Ottawa was seen as a compromise between Montreal, Toronto, [Kingston](/wiki/Kingston,_Ontario "Kingston, Ontario") and Quebec City, which were all vying to become the young nation's official capital. Ottawa retained the status as capital of Canada when the Province of Canada joined with [Nova Scotia](/wiki/Nova_Scotia "Nova Scotia") and [New Brunswick](/wiki/New_Brunswick "New Brunswick") to form the Dominion of Canada in 1867.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

An [internment camp](/wiki/Internment_camp "Internment camp") was set up at Immigration Hall in Montreal from August 1914 to November 1918.[[72]](#cite_note-75)

After [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), the [prohibition](/wiki/Prohibition "Prohibition") movement in the United States led to Montreal becoming a destination for Americans looking for [alcohol](/wiki/Alcoholic_beverage "Alcoholic beverage").[[73]](#cite_note-Telegraph-76) Unemployment remained high in the city and was exacerbated by the [Stock Market Crash of 1929](/wiki/Wall_Street_Crash_1929 "Wall Street Crash 1929") and the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression").[[74]](#cite_note-77)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3a/Anti-conscription_parade_at_Victoria_Square.jpg/250px-Anti-conscription_parade_at_Victoria_Square.jpg)](/wiki/File:Anti-conscription_parade_at_Victoria_Square.jpg)

An anti-[conscription](/wiki/Conscription "Conscription") rally in Montreal, 1917. During both [World Wars](/wiki/World_Wars "World Wars"), the city saw protest against the implementation of conscription.

During [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), Mayor [Camillien Houde](/wiki/Camillien_Houde "Camillien Houde") protested against conscription and urged Montrealers to disobey the federal government's registry of all men and women.[[75]](#cite_note-MTAU-78) The federal government, part of the [Allied forces](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_II "Allies of World War II"), was furious over Houde's stand and held him in a prison camp until 1944.[[76]](#cite_note-79) That year, the government decided to institute conscription to expand the armed forces and fight the [Axis powers](/wiki/Axis_powers "Axis powers"). (See [Conscription Crisis of 1944](/wiki/Conscription_Crisis_of_1944 "Conscription Crisis of 1944").)[[75]](#cite_note-MTAU-78)

Montreal was the official residence of the [Luxembourg](/wiki/Luxembourg "Luxembourg") royal family in exile during World War II.[[77]](#cite_note-80)

By 1951, Montreal's population had surpassed one million.[[78]](#cite_note-81) However, Toronto's growth had begun challenging Montreal's status as the economic capital of Canada. Indeed, the volume of stocks traded at the [Toronto Stock Exchange](/wiki/Toronto_Stock_Exchange "Toronto Stock Exchange") had already surpassed that traded at the [Montreal Stock Exchange](/wiki/Montreal_Stock_Exchange "Montreal Stock Exchange") in the 1940s.[[79]](#cite_note-82) The [Saint Lawrence Seaway](/wiki/Saint_Lawrence_Seaway "Saint Lawrence Seaway") opened in 1959, allowing vessels to bypass Montreal. In time, this development led to the end of the city's economic dominance as businesses moved to other areas.[[80]](#cite_note-83) During the 1960s, there was continued growth as Canada's tallest skyscrapers, new expressways and the subway system known as the [Montreal Metro](/wiki/Montreal_Metro "Montreal Metro") were finished during this time. Montreal also held the World's Fair of 1967, better known as [Expo67](/wiki/Expo67 "Expo67").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/Stephane_Prefontaine%2C_Sandra_Henderson_1976b.jpg/250px-Stephane_Prefontaine%2C_Sandra_Henderson_1976b.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stephane_Prefontaine,_Sandra_Henderson_1976b.jpg)

Lighting of the [Olympic Torch](/wiki/Olympic_Torch "Olympic Torch") inside Montreal's [Olympic Stadium](/wiki/Olympic_Stadium_(Montreal) "Olympic Stadium (Montreal)"). The city hosted the [1976 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1976_Summer_Olympics "1976 Summer Olympics").

The 1970s ushered in a period of wide-ranging social and political changes, stemming largely from the concerns of the [French-speaking](/wiki/French_Canadians "French Canadians") majority about the conservation of their culture and language, given the traditional predominance of the [English Canadian](/wiki/English_Canadians "English Canadians") minority in the business arena.[[81]](#cite_note-84) The [October Crisis](/wiki/October_Crisis "October Crisis") and the 1976 election of the [Parti Québécois](/wiki/Parti_Qu%C3%A9b%C3%A9cois "Parti Québécois"), which supported sovereign status for Quebec, resulted in the departure of many businesses and people from the city.[[82]](#cite_note-RoughGuide-85) In 1976, Montreal hosted the [Summer Olympics](/wiki/1976_Summer_Olympics "1976 Summer Olympics"). While the event brought the city international prestige and attention, the [Olympic Stadium](/wiki/Olympic_Stadium_(Montreal) "Olympic Stadium (Montreal)") built for the event resulted in massive debt for the city.[[83]](#cite_note-IOC-86) During the 1980s and early 1990s, Montreal experienced a slower rate of economic growth than many other major Canadian cities. Montreal was the site of the 1989 [École Polytechnique massacre](/wiki/%C3%89cole_Polytechnique_massacre "École Polytechnique massacre"), one of Canada's worst [mass shootings](/wiki/Mass_shooting "Mass shooting"), where 25-year-old [Marc Lépine](/wiki/Marc_L%C3%A9pine "Marc Lépine") shot and killed 14 people, all of them women, and wounded 14 other people before shooting himself at [École Polytechnique](/wiki/Polytechnique_Montr%C3%A9al "Polytechnique Montréal").

Montreal was [merged](/wiki/Montreal_Merger "Montreal Merger") with the 27 surrounding municipalities on the Island of Montreal on January 1, 2002, creating a unified city encompassing the entire island. There was substantial resistance from the suburbs to the merger, with the perception being that it was forced on the mostly English suburbs by the Parti Québécois. As expected, this move proved unpopular and several mergers were later rescinded. Several former municipalities, totalling 13% of the population of the island, voted to leave the unified city in separate [referendums](/wiki/Referendum "Referendum") in June 2004. The demerger took place on January 1, 2006, leaving 15 municipalities on the island, including Montreal. Demerged municipalities remain affiliated with the city through an agglomeration council that collects taxes from them to pay for numerous shared services.[[84]](#cite_note-Agglomeration_council-87) The 2002 mergers were not the first in the city's history. Montreal annexed 27 other cities, towns and villages beginning with [Hochelaga](/wiki/Mercier%E2%80%93Hochelaga-Maisonneuve#History "Mercier–Hochelaga-Maisonneuve") in 1883, with the last prior to 2002 being [Pointe-aux-Trembles](/wiki/Pointe-aux-Trembles "Pointe-aux-Trembles") in 1982.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/16/Montreal_Skyline_from_Mont_Royal.jpg/500px-Montreal_Skyline_from_Mont_Royal.jpg)](/wiki/File:Montreal_Skyline_from_Mont_Royal.jpg)

The Montreal Skyline in 2019

The 21st century has brought with it a revival of the city's economic and cultural landscape. Helping Montreal continue to grow are: the construction of new residential skyscrapers, two super-hospitals (the [Centre hospitalier de l'Université de Montréal](/wiki/Centre_hospitalier_de_l%27Universit%C3%A9_de_Montr%C3%A9al "Centre hospitalier de l'Université de Montréal") and [McGill University Health Centre](/wiki/McGill_University_Health_Centre "McGill University Health Centre")), the creation of the [Quartier des Spectacles](/wiki/Quartier_des_Spectacles "Quartier des Spectacles"), reconstruction of the [Turcot Interchange](/wiki/Turcot_Interchange "Turcot Interchange"), reconfiguration of the Decarie and Dorval interchanges, construction of the new [Réseau express métropolitain](/wiki/R%C3%A9seau_express_m%C3%A9tropolitain "Réseau express métropolitain"), gentrification of [Griffintown](/wiki/Griffintown "Griffintown"), subway line extensions and the purchase of new subway cars, the complete revitalization and expansion of [Trudeau International Airport](/wiki/Montr%C3%A9al%E2%80%93Trudeau_International_Airport "Montréal–Trudeau International Airport"), the completion of [Quebec Autoroute 30](/wiki/Quebec_Autoroute_30 "Quebec Autoroute 30"), the reconstruction of the [Champlain Bridge](/wiki/Champlain_Bridge,_Montreal "Champlain Bridge, Montreal") and the construction of a new toll bridge to Laval.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Montreal&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Geography")]

Main article: [Geography of Montreal](/wiki/Geography_of_Montreal "Geography of Montreal")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/Montreal_Canada_from_ISS014.jpg/250px-Montreal_Canada_from_ISS014.jpg)](/wiki/File:Montreal_Canada_from_ISS014.jpg)

The [island of Montreal](/wiki/Island_of_Montreal "Island of Montreal") at the confluence of the [Saint Lawrence](/wiki/Saint_Lawrence_River "Saint Lawrence River") and [Ottawa](/wiki/Ottawa_River "Ottawa River") rivers

Montreal is in the southwest of the province of Quebec. The city covers most of the Island of Montreal at the confluence of the Saint Lawrence and Ottawa Rivers. The port of Montreal lies at one end of the Saint Lawrence Seaway, the river gateway that stretches from the [Great Lakes](/wiki/Great_Lakes "Great Lakes") to the Atlantic.[[85]](#cite_note-Great_Canadian_Rivers-88) Montreal is defined by its location between the Saint Lawrence river to its south and the [Rivière des Prairies](/wiki/Rivi%C3%A8re_des_Prairies "Rivière des Prairies") to its north. The city is named after the most prominent geographical feature on the island, a three-head mountain called Mount Royal, topped at 232 metres (761 ft) [above sea level](/wiki/Above_mean_sea_level "Above mean sea level").[[86]](#cite_note-Natural_Resources_Canada-89)

Montreal is at the centre of the [Montreal Metropolitan Community](/wiki/Greater_Montreal "Greater Montreal"), and is bordered by the city of [Laval](/wiki/Laval,_Quebec "Laval, Quebec") to the north; [Longueuil](/wiki/Longueuil "Longueuil"), [Saint-Lambert](/wiki/Saint-Lambert,_Quebec "Saint-Lambert, Quebec"), [Brossard](/wiki/Brossard "Brossard"), and other municipalities to the south; [Repentigny](/wiki/Repentigny,_Quebec "Repentigny, Quebec") to the east and the [West Island](/wiki/West_Island "West Island") municipalities to the west. The enclaves of [Westmount](/wiki/Westmount,_Quebec "Westmount, Quebec"), [Montreal West](/wiki/Montreal_West,_Quebec "Montreal West, Quebec"), [Hampstead](/wiki/Hampstead,_Quebec "Hampstead, Quebec"), [Côte Saint-Luc](/wiki/C%C3%B4te_Saint-Luc "Côte Saint-Luc"), and the [Town of Mount Royal](/wiki/Mount_Royal,_Quebec "Mount Royal, Quebec") are all surrounded by Montreal, while [Montreal East](/wiki/Montr%C3%A9al-Est,_Quebec "Montréal-Est, Quebec") is surrounded by Montreal on three sides.[[87]](#cite_note-City_Map-90)

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Montreal&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Climate")]

Montreal is classified as a [warm-summer](/wiki/Warm-summer_humid_continental_climate "Warm-summer humid continental climate") [humid continental climate](/wiki/Humid_continental_climate "Humid continental climate") zone (Köppen climate classification: *Dfb*), bordering the [hot-summer](/wiki/Hot-summer_humid_continental_climate "Hot-summer humid continental climate") humid continental climate ([Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification"): *Dfa*)[[88]](#cite_note-McGill-91). Summers are hot and humid with a daily maximum average of 26 to 27 °C (79 to 81 °F) in July; temperatures in excess of 30 °C (86 °F) are common. Conversely, cold fronts can bring crisp, drier and windy weather in the early and later parts of summer.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6f/Montreal_Winter_Snowing.jpg/250px-Montreal_Winter_Snowing.jpg)](/wiki/File:Montreal_Winter_Snowing.jpg)

Winters in Montreal bring cold, snowy, windy, and at times, icy weather.

Winter brings cold, snowy, windy, and, at times, icy weather, with a daily average ranging from −10.5 to −9 °C (13.1 to 15.8 °F) in January. However, some winter days rise above freezing, allowing for rain on an average of 4 days in January and February each. Usually, snow covering some or all bare ground lasts on average from the first or second week of December until March.[[89]](#cite_note-CurrentResults.com-92) While the air temperature has not fallen below −30 °C (−22 °F) for more than 30 years,[[90]](#cite_note-Montreal_Weather_Data-93) the [wind chill](/wiki/Wind_chill "Wind chill") often makes the temperature feel this low to exposed skin.

Spring and fall are pleasantly mild but prone to drastic temperature changes; spring even more so than fall.[[91]](#cite_note-94) Late season heat waves as well as "[Indian summers](/wiki/Indian_summer "Indian summer")" are possible. Early and late season snow storms can occur in November and March, and more rarely in April. Montreal is generally snow free from late April to late October. However, snow can fall in early to mid-October as well as early May on rare occasions.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The lowest temperature in Environment Canada's books was −37.8 °C (−36 °F) on January 15, 1957, and the highest temperature was 37.6 °C (99.7 °F) on August 1, 1975, both at [Dorval International Airport](/wiki/Montr%C3%A9al%E2%80%93Pierre_Elliott_Trudeau_International_Airport "Montréal–Pierre Elliott Trudeau International Airport").[[92]](#cite_note-95)

Before modern weather record keeping (which dates back to 1871 for McGill),[[93]](#cite_note-96) a minimum temperature almost 5 degrees lower was recorded at 7 a.m. on January 10, 1859, where it registered at −42 °C (−44 °F).[[94]](#cite_note-97)

Annual precipitation is around 1,000 mm (39 in), including an average of about 210 cm (83 in) of snowfall, which occurs from November through March. Thunderstorms are common from late spring through summer to early fall; additionally, tropical storms or their remnants can cause heavy rains and gales. Montreal averages 2,050 hours of sunshine annually, with summer being the sunniest season, though slightly wetter than the others in terms of total precipitation—mostly from thunderstorms.[[95]](#cite_note-PET_stats-98)

## Architecture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Montreal&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Architecture")]

Main article: [Architecture of Montreal](/wiki/Architecture_of_Montreal "Architecture of Montreal")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/94/Vieux-Montr%C3%A9al_%28525744602%29.jpg/250px-Vieux-Montr%C3%A9al_%28525744602%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Vieux-Montr%C3%A9al_(525744602).jpg)

Many colonial era buildings can be found in [Old Montreal](/wiki/Old_Montreal "Old Montreal") with several dating as far back as the late 17th century.

For over a century and a half, Montreal was the industrial and financial centre of Canada.[[102]](#cite_note-105) This legacy has left a variety of buildings including factories, [elevators](/wiki/Grain_elevators "Grain elevators"), [warehouses](/wiki/Warehouses "Warehouses"), mills, and [refineries](/wiki/Refineries "Refineries"), that today provide an invaluable insight into the city's history, especially in the downtown area and the [Old Port](/wiki/Old_Port_of_Montreal "Old Port of Montreal") area. There are 50 [National Historic Sites of Canada](/wiki/List_of_National_Historic_Sites_of_Canada_in_Montreal "List of National Historic Sites of Canada in Montreal"), more than any other city.[[103]](#cite_note-106)

Some of the city's earliest still-standing buildings date back to the late 17th and early 18th centuries. Although most are clustered around the [Old Montreal](/wiki/Old_Montreal "Old Montreal") area, such as the [Sulpician Seminary](/wiki/Saint-Sulpice_Seminary_(Montreal) "Saint-Sulpice Seminary (Montreal)") adjacent to [Notre-Dame Basilica](/wiki/Notre-Dame_Basilica_(Montreal) "Notre-Dame Basilica (Montreal)") that dates back to 1687, and [Château Ramezay](/wiki/Ch%C3%A2teau_Ramezay "Château Ramezay"), which was built in 1705, examples of early colonial architecture are dotted throughout the city. Situated in Lachine, the [Le Ber-Le Moyne House](/wiki/LeBer-LeMoyne_House "LeBer-LeMoyne House") is the oldest complete building in the city, built between 1669 and 1671. In [Point St. Charles](/wiki/Point_St._Charles "Point St. Charles"), visitors can see the [Maison Saint-Gabriel](/wiki/Maison_Saint-Gabriel "Maison Saint-Gabriel"), which can trace its history back to 1698.[[104]](#cite_note-107) There are many historic buildings in Old Montreal in their original form: Notre-Dame Basilica, [Bonsecours Market](/wiki/Bonsecours_Market "Bonsecours Market"), and the 19th‑century headquarters of all major Canadian banks on [St. James Street](/wiki/Saint_Jacques_Street "Saint Jacques Street") (French: *Rue Saint Jacques*). Montreal's earliest buildings are characterized by their uniquely French influence and grey stone construction.[[105]](#cite_note-108)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fa/Habitat-67_side_view_with_holes_visible_in_the_structure.jpg/250px-Habitat-67_side_view_with_holes_visible_in_the_structure.jpg)](/wiki/File:Habitat-67_side_view_with_holes_visible_in_the_structure.jpg)

[Habitat 67](/wiki/Habitat_67 "Habitat 67") is a model community and housing complex developed for [Expo 67](/wiki/Expo_67 "Expo 67") [World Fair](/wiki/World_Fair "World Fair").

A few notable examples of the city's 20th-century architecture include [Saint Joseph's Oratory](/wiki/Saint_Joseph%27s_Oratory "Saint Joseph's Oratory"), completed in 1967, [Ernest Cormier](/wiki/Ernest_Cormier "Ernest Cormier")'s [Art Deco](/wiki/Art_Deco "Art Deco") [Université de Montréal](/wiki/Universit%C3%A9_de_Montr%C3%A9al "Université de Montréal") main building, the landmark [Place Ville Marie](/wiki/Place_Ville_Marie "Place Ville Marie") office tower, and the controversial [Olympic Stadium](/wiki/Olympic_Stadium_(Montreal) "Olympic Stadium (Montreal)") and surrounding structures. Pavilions designed for the 1967 International and Universal Exposition, popularly known as [Expo 67](/wiki/Expo_67 "Expo 67"), featured a wide range of architectural designs. Though most pavilions were temporary structures, several have become landmarks, including [Buckminster Fuller](/wiki/Buckminster_Fuller "Buckminster Fuller")'s [geodesic dome](/wiki/Geodesic_dome "Geodesic dome") U.S. Pavilion, now the [Montreal Biosphere](/wiki/Montreal_Biosphere "Montreal Biosphere"), and [Moshe Safdie](/wiki/Moshe_Safdie "Moshe Safdie")'s striking [Habitat 67](/wiki/Habitat_67 "Habitat 67") apartment complex.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The [Montreal Metro](/wiki/Montreal_Metro "Montreal Metro") has public artwork by some of the biggest names in [Quebec culture](/wiki/Culture_of_Quebec "Culture of Quebec").[[106]](#cite_note-109)

In 2006, Montreal was named a [UNESCO City of Design](/wiki/Design_Cities_(UNESCO) "Design Cities (UNESCO)"), one of only three design capitals in the world (the others being [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin "Berlin") and [Buenos Aires](/wiki/Buenos_Aires "Buenos Aires")).[[25]](#cite_note-UNESCO-27) This distinguished title recognizes Montreal's design community. Since 2005, the city has been home to the [International Council of Graphic Design Associations](/wiki/International_Council_of_Graphic_Design_Associations "International Council of Graphic Design Associations") (Icograda)[[107]](#cite_note-ICOGRADA-110) and the International Design Alliance (IDA).[[108]](#cite_note-BNET-111)

The [Underground City](/wiki/Underground_City,_Montreal "Underground City, Montreal") (officially RÉSO), an important tourist attraction, is an underground network connecting shopping centres, pedestrian thoroughfares, universities, hotels, restaurants, bistros, subway stations and more, in and around downtown with 32 km (20 mi) of tunnels over 12 km2 (4.6 sq mi) in the most densely populated part of Montreal.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/ArrondissementsQuartiersMontreal_200802.svg/250px-ArrondissementsQuartiersMontreal_200802.svg.png)](/wiki/File:ArrondissementsQuartiersMontreal_200802.svg)

Map of boroughs and neighbourhoods on the island of Montreal

The city is composed of 19 large [boroughs](/wiki/Boroughs_of_Montreal "Boroughs of Montreal"), subdivided into neighbourhoods.[[109]](#cite_note-Bits-112)
The boroughs are:
[Côte-des-Neiges–Notre-Dame-de-Grâce](/wiki/C%C3%B4te-des-Neiges%E2%80%93Notre-Dame-de-Gr%C3%A2ce "Côte-des-Neiges–Notre-Dame-de-Grâce"), [Le Plateau-Mont-Royal](/wiki/Le_Plateau-Mont-Royal "Le Plateau-Mont-Royal") (The Plateau Mount Royal), [Outremont](/wiki/Outremont_(borough) "Outremont (borough)") and [Ville-Marie](/wiki/Ville-Marie_(Montreal) "Ville-Marie (Montreal)") in the centre; [Mercier–Hochelaga-Maisonneuve](/wiki/Mercier%E2%80%93Hochelaga-Maisonneuve "Mercier–Hochelaga-Maisonneuve"), [Rosemont–La Petite-Patrie](/wiki/Rosemont%E2%80%93La_Petite-Patrie "Rosemont–La Petite-Patrie") and [Villeray–Saint-Michel–Parc-Extension](/wiki/Villeray%E2%80%93Saint-Michel%E2%80%93Parc-Extension "Villeray–Saint-Michel–Parc-Extension") in the east; [Anjou](/wiki/Anjou_(borough) "Anjou (borough)"), [Montréal-Nord](/wiki/Montr%C3%A9al-Nord "Montréal-Nord"), [Rivière-des-Prairies–Pointe-aux-Trembles](/wiki/Rivi%C3%A8re-des-Prairies%E2%80%93Pointe-aux-Trembles "Rivière-des-Prairies–Pointe-aux-Trembles") and [Saint-Léonard](/wiki/Saint-L%C3%A9onard,_Quebec "Saint-Léonard, Quebec") in the northeast; [Ahuntsic-Cartierville](/wiki/Ahuntsic-Cartierville "Ahuntsic-Cartierville"), [L'Île-Bizard–Sainte-Geneviève](/wiki/L%27%C3%8Ele-Bizard%E2%80%93Sainte-Genevi%C3%A8ve "L'Île-Bizard–Sainte-Geneviève"), [Pierrefonds-Roxboro](/wiki/Pierrefonds-Roxboro "Pierrefonds-Roxboro") and [Saint-Laurent](/wiki/Saint-Laurent_(borough) "Saint-Laurent (borough)") in the northwest; and [Lachine](/wiki/Lachine_(borough) "Lachine (borough)"), [LaSalle](/wiki/LaSalle_(borough) "LaSalle (borough)"), [Le Sud-Ouest](/wiki/Le_Sud-Ouest "Le Sud-Ouest") (The Southwest) and [Verdun](/wiki/Verdun_(borough) "Verdun (borough)") in the south.[[110]](#cite_note-Boroughs-113)

Many of these boroughs were independent cities that were forced to merge with Montreal in January 2002 following the [2002 municipal reorganization of Montreal](/wiki/2002-2006_municipal_reorganization_of_Montreal "2002-2006 municipal reorganization of Montreal").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/Montreal_August_2017_05.jpg/250px-Montreal_August_2017_05.jpg)](/wiki/File:Montreal_August_2017_05.jpg)

A view of [Downtown Montreal](/wiki/Downtown_Montreal "Downtown Montreal") from [Mount Royal](/wiki/Mount_Royal "Mount Royal"). Many neighbourhoods, including downtown, are in the borough of [Ville-Marie](/wiki/Ville-Marie,_Montreal "Ville-Marie, Montreal").

The borough with the most neighbourhoods is Ville-Marie, which includes downtown, the historic district of Old Montreal, [Chinatown](/wiki/Chinatown_(Montreal) "Chinatown (Montreal)"), the [Gay Village](/wiki/Gay_Village,_Montreal "Gay Village, Montreal"), the [Latin Quarter](/wiki/Quartier_Latin,_Montreal "Quartier Latin, Montreal"), the gentrified [Quartier international](/wiki/Quartier_international_de_Montr%C3%A9al "Quartier international de Montréal") and [Cité Multimédia](/wiki/Cit%C3%A9_Multim%C3%A9dia "Cité Multimédia") as well as the [Quartier des spectacles](/wiki/Quartier_des_spectacles "Quartier des spectacles") which is under development.[*[as of?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] Other neighbourhoods of interest in the borough include the affluent [Golden Square Mile](/wiki/Golden_Square_Mile "Golden Square Mile") neighbourhood at the foot of Mount Royal and the [Shaughnessy Village](/wiki/Shaughnessy_Village "Shaughnessy Village")/[Concordia U](/wiki/Quartier_Concordia "Quartier Concordia") area home to thousands of students at [Concordia University](/wiki/Concordia_University_(Montreal) "Concordia University (Montreal)"). The borough also comprises most of [Mount Royal Park](/wiki/Mount_Royal_Park "Mount Royal Park"), [Saint Helen's Island](/wiki/Saint_Helen%27s_Island "Saint Helen's Island"), and [Notre-Dame Island](/wiki/%C3%8Ele_Notre-Dame "Île Notre-Dame").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The Plateau Mount Royal borough was a working class francophone area. The largest neighbourhood is [the Plateau](/wiki/The_Plateau "The Plateau") (not to be confused with the whole borough), which was undergoing considerable gentrification as of 2009,[[111]](#cite_note-114) and a 2001 study deemed it as Canada's most creative neighbourhood because artists comprise 8% of its labour force.[[112]](#cite_note-Hill-115) The neighbourhood of [Mile End](/wiki/Mile_End,_Montreal "Mile End, Montreal") in the northwestern part of the borough has been a very multicultural area of the city, and features two of Montreal's well-known [bagel establishments](/wiki/Montreal-style_bagel "Montreal-style bagel"), [St-Viateur Bagel](/wiki/St-Viateur_Bagel "St-Viateur Bagel") and [Fairmount Bagel](/wiki/Fairmount_Bagel "Fairmount Bagel"). The [McGill Ghetto](/wiki/McGill_Ghetto "McGill Ghetto") is in the extreme southwestern portion of the borough, its name being derived from the fact that it is home to thousands of [McGill University](/wiki/McGill_University "McGill University") students and faculty members.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The Southwest borough was home to much of the city's industry during the late 19th and early-to-mid 20th century. The borough included [Goose Village](/wiki/Goose_Village,_Montreal "Goose Village, Montreal") and was historically home to the traditionally working-class [Irish](/wiki/Irish_Quebecer "Irish Quebecer") neighbourhoods of [Griffintown](/wiki/Griffintown "Griffintown") and [Point Saint Charles](/wiki/Pointe-Saint-Charles "Pointe-Saint-Charles") as well as the low-income neighbourhoods of [Saint Henri](/wiki/Saint-Henri "Saint-Henri") and [Little Burgundy](/wiki/Little_Burgundy "Little Burgundy").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Other notable neighbourhoods include the multicultural areas of [Notre-Dame-de-Grâce](/wiki/Notre-Dame-de-Gr%C3%A2ce "Notre-Dame-de-Grâce") and [Côte-des-Neiges](/wiki/C%C3%B4te-des-Neiges "Côte-des-Neiges") in the Côte-des-Neiges–Notre-Dame-de-Grace borough, and [Little Italy](/wiki/Little_Italy,_Montreal "Little Italy, Montreal") in the borough of Rosemont–La Petite-Patrie and [Hochelaga-Maisonneuve](/wiki/Hochelaga-Maisonneuve "Hochelaga-Maisonneuve"), home of the [Olympic Stadium](/wiki/Olympic_Stadium,_Montreal "Olympic Stadium, Montreal") in the borough of Mercier–Hochelaga-Maisonneuve.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/88/Place_Jacques-Cartier_Jan_2006.jpg/250px-Place_Jacques-Cartier_Jan_2006.jpg)](/wiki/File:Place_Jacques-Cartier_Jan_2006.jpg)

[Place Jacques-Cartier](/wiki/Place_Jacques-Cartier "Place Jacques-Cartier") is a major [public square](/wiki/Public_square "Public square") and attraction in [Old Montreal](/wiki/Old_Montreal "Old Montreal").

Old Montreal is a historic area southeast of downtown containing many attractions such as the [Old Port of Montreal](/wiki/Old_Port_of_Montreal "Old Port of Montreal"), Place Jacques-Cartier, [Montreal City Hall](/wiki/Montreal_City_Hall "Montreal City Hall"), the [Bonsecours Market](/wiki/Bonsecours_Market "Bonsecours Market"), Place d'Armes, [Pointe-à-Callière Museum](/wiki/Pointe-%C3%A0-Calli%C3%A8re_Museum "Pointe-à-Callière Museum"), the Notre-Dame de Montréal Basilica, and the [Montreal Science Centre](/wiki/Montreal_Science_Centre "Montreal Science Centre").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Architecture and cobbled streets in Old Montreal have been maintained or restored. Old Montreal is accessible from the downtown core via the [underground city](/wiki/Underground_city,_Montreal "Underground city, Montreal") and is served by several [STM](/wiki/Soci%C3%A9t%C3%A9_de_transport_de_Montr%C3%A9al "Société de transport de Montréal") bus routes and Metro stations, ferries to the South Shore and a network of bicycle paths.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The riverside area adjacent to Old Montreal is known as the Old Port. It was once the site of the [Port of Montreal](/wiki/Port_of_Montreal "Port of Montreal"), but its shipping operations have been moved to a larger site downstream, leaving the former location as a recreational and historical area maintained by [Parks Canada](/wiki/Parks_Canada "Parks Canada"). The new Port of Montreal is Canada's largest container port and the largest inland port on Earth.[[113]](#cite_note-116)

### Mount Royal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Montreal&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Mount Royal")]

Main article: [Mount Royal](/wiki/Mount_Royal "Mount Royal")

The mountain is the site of Mount Royal Park, one of Montreal's largest [greenspaces](/wiki/Open_space_reserve "Open space reserve"). The park, most of which is wooded, was designed by [Frederick Law Olmsted](/wiki/Frederick_Law_Olmsted "Frederick Law Olmsted"), who also designed New York's [Central Park](/wiki/Central_Park "Central Park"), and was inaugurated in 1876.[[114]](#cite_note-LesAmisDeLaMontagne-117)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d8/Mtroyal.jpg/250px-Mtroyal.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mtroyal.jpg)

View of [Mont-Royal](/wiki/Mont-Royal "Mont-Royal")'s eastern slope from the [George-Étienne Cartier Monument](/wiki/George-%C3%89tienne_Cartier_Monument "George-Étienne Cartier Monument"). The park is one of Montreal's largest [open space reserves](/wiki/Open_space_reserve "Open space reserve").

The park contains two [belvederes](/wiki/Belvedere_(structure) "Belvedere (structure)"), the more prominent of which is the Kondiaronk Belvedere, a semicircular plaza with a [chalet](/wiki/Chalet "Chalet") overlooking Downtown Montreal. Other features of the park are Beaver Lake, a small man-made lake, a short [ski](/wiki/Skiing "Skiing") [slope](/wiki/Ski_slope "Ski slope"), a [sculpture garden](/wiki/Sculpture_garden "Sculpture garden"), Smith House, an [interpretive centre](/wiki/Interpretive_centre "Interpretive centre"), and a well-known [monument to Sir George-Étienne Cartier](/wiki/George-%C3%89tienne_Cartier_Monument "George-Étienne Cartier Monument"). The park hosts athletic, tourist and cultural activities.

The mountain is home to two major cemeteries, Notre-Dame-des-Neiges (founded in 1854) and Mount Royal (1852). [Mount Royal Cemetery](/wiki/Mount_Royal_Cemetery "Mount Royal Cemetery") is a 165 acres (67 ha) terraced cemetery on the north slope of Mount Royal in the borough of Outremont. [Notre Dame des Neiges Cemetery](/wiki/Notre_Dame_des_Neiges_Cemetery "Notre Dame des Neiges Cemetery") is much larger, predominantly French-Canadian and officially Catholic.[[115]](#cite_note-118) More than 900,000 people are buried there.[[116]](#cite_note-119)

Mount Royal Cemetery contains more than 162,000 graves and is the final resting place for a number of notable Canadians. It includes a veterans section with several soldiers who were awarded the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire")'s highest military honour, the [Victoria Cross](/wiki/Victoria_Cross "Victoria Cross"). In 1901, the Mount Royal Cemetery Company established the first crematorium in Canada.[[117]](#cite_note-MountRoyalCemetery-120)

The first [cross](/wiki/Mount_Royal_Cross "Mount Royal Cross") on the mountain was placed there in 1643 by Paul Chomedey de Maisonneuve, the founder of the city, in fulfilment of a vow he made to the [Virgin Mary](/wiki/Mary,_mother_of_Jesus "Mary, mother of Jesus") when [praying](/wiki/Prayer "Prayer") to her to stop a disastrous flood.[[114]](#cite_note-LesAmisDeLaMontagne-117) Today, the mountain is crowned by a 31.4 m-high (103 ft) illuminated cross, installed in 1924 by the [John the Baptist Society](/wiki/Soci%C3%A9t%C3%A9_Saint-Jean-Baptiste "Société Saint-Jean-Baptiste") and now owned by the city.[[114]](#cite_note-LesAmisDeLaMontagne-117) It was converted to [fibre optic](/wiki/Optical_fibre "Optical fibre") light in 1992.[[114]](#cite_note-LesAmisDeLaMontagne-117) The new system can turn the lights red, blue, or purple, the last of which is used as a sign of mourning between the death of the Pope and the election of the next.[[118]](#cite_note-Hour-121)

In 2024, Elie-Wiesel Park was inaugurated on de Courtrai Avenue near [Décarie Boulevard](/wiki/D%C3%A9carie_Boulevard "Décarie Boulevard") and Westbury Avenue in the [Snowdon](/wiki/Snowdon,_Montreal "Snowdon, Montreal") district in [Côte-des-Neiges–Notre-Dame-de-Grâce](/wiki/C%C3%B4te-des-Neiges%E2%80%93Notre-Dame-de-Gr%C3%A2ce "Côte-des-Neiges–Notre-Dame-de-Grâce") in honour of [Elie Wiesel](/wiki/Elie_Wiesel "Elie Wiesel"), the [Holocaust survivor](/wiki/Holocaust_survivor "Holocaust survivor"), writer, professor, and [Nobel Peace Prize](/wiki/Nobel_Peace_Prize "Nobel Peace Prize") winner; it has a playground, relaxation area, walking trails, furniture, green spaces, and unique water features.[[119]](#cite_note-122)

In the [2021 Canadian census](/wiki/2021_Canadian_census "2021 Canadian census") conducted by [Statistics Canada](/wiki/Statistics_Canada "Statistics Canada"), Montreal had a population of 1,762,949 living in 816,338 of its 878,542 total private dwellings, a change of 3.4% from its 2016 population of 1,704,694. With a land area of 364.74 km2 (140.83 sq mi), it had a population density of 4,833.4/km2 (12,518.6/sq mi) in 2021.[[7]](#cite_note-pop_city_cma_pc-7)

At the same census Statistics Canada the [Census metropolitan area](/wiki/Census_geographic_units_of_Canada#Census_metropolitan_areas "Census geographic units of Canada") had a population of 4,291,732, an increase of 4.6% from its 2016 population of 4,104,074.[[7]](#cite_note-pop_city_cma_pc-7)

The 2021 census recorded 270,430 (15.3%) children between 0 and 14 years of age, while 295,475 (16.8%) were 65 and over.[[7]](#cite_note-pop_city_cma_pc-7)

According to Statistics Canada, by 2030, the Greater Montreal Area is expected to number 5,275,000 with 1,722,000 being visible minorities.[[120]](#cite_note-123)

### Ethnicity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Montreal&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Ethnicity")]

People of [European ethnicities](/wiki/European_ethnic_groups "European ethnic groups") formed the largest cluster of ethnic groups. The largest reported European ethnicities in the 2006 census were [French](/wiki/French_people "French people") (23%), [Italians](/wiki/Italian_people "Italian people") (10%), [Irish](/wiki/Irish_people "Irish people") (5%), [English](/wiki/English_people "English people") (4%), [Scottish](/wiki/Scottish_people "Scottish people") (3%), and [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_people "Spanish people") (2%).[[121]](#cite_note-european_origins-124)

The [panethnic](/wiki/Panethnicity "Panethnicity") breakdown of the city of Montreal as per the [2021 census](/wiki/2021_Canadian_census "2021 Canadian census") was [European](/wiki/European_Canadians "European Canadians")[[b]](#cite_note-European-125) (1,038,940 residents or 60.3% of the population), [African](/wiki/African-Canadian "African-Canadian") (198,610; 11.5%), [Middle Eastern](/wiki/Middle_Eastern_Canadians "Middle Eastern Canadians")[[c]](#cite_note-MiddleEastern-126) (159,435; 9.3%), [South Asian](/wiki/South_Asian_Canadians "South Asian Canadians") (79,670; 4.6%), [Latin American](/wiki/Latin_American_Canadians "Latin American Canadians") (78,150; 4.5%), [Southeast Asian](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia")[[d]](#cite_note-SoutheastAsian-127) (65,260; 3.8%), [East Asian](/wiki/East_Asian_Canadians "East Asian Canadians")[[e]](#cite_note-EastAsian-128) (64,825; 3.8%), [Indigenous](/wiki/Indigenous_Canadian "Indigenous Canadian") (15,315; 0.9%), and Other/[Multiracial](/wiki/Multiracial_people "Multiracial people")[[f]](#cite_note-Other-129) (23,010; 1.3%).[[122]](#cite_note-2021CensusCity-130)

[Visible minorities](/wiki/Visible_minorities "Visible minorities") comprised 38.8% of the city of Montreal population in the [2021 census](/wiki/Canada_2021_Census "Canada 2021 Census").[[122]](#cite_note-2021CensusCity-130) The five most numerous visible minorities are [Black Canadians](/wiki/Black_Canadians "Black Canadians") (11.5%), [Arab Canadians](/wiki/Arab_Canadians "Arab Canadians") (8.2%), South Asian Canadians (4.6%), Latin Americans (4.5%), and [Chinese Canadians](/wiki/Chinese_Canadians "Chinese Canadians") (3.3%).[[122]](#cite_note-2021CensusCity-130) Furthermore, some 27.2% of the population [Greater Montreal](/wiki/Greater_Montreal "Greater Montreal") are members of a visible minority group as of 2021,[[123]](#cite_note-2021CensusMetroB-131) up from 5.2% in 1981.[[124]](#cite_note-132) Visible minorities are defined by the [Canadian Employment Equity Act](/wiki/Canadian_Employment_Equity_Act "Canadian Employment Equity Act") as "persons, other than [Aboriginals](/wiki/Aboriginal_peoples_of_Canada "Aboriginal peoples of Canada"), who are non-white in colour".[[125]](#cite_note-133)

### Language

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Montreal&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Language")]

As of the 2021 Census,[[122]](#cite_note-2021CensusCity-130) 47.0% of Montreal residents spoke [French](/wiki/French_language "French language") alone as a first language, while 13.0% spoke [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") alone. Some 2% spoke both English and French as first languages, 2.6% spoke both French and a non-official language and 1.5% spoke both English and a non-official language. 0.8% of residents spoke English, French and a non-official language as first languages. 32.8% of residents spoke one non-official language as a first language, and 0.3% spoke multiple non-official languages as first languages. The most common were [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic "Arabic") (5.7%), [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language") (4.6%), [Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language") (3.3%), [Chinese languages](/wiki/Sinitic_languages "Sinitic languages") (2.7%), [Haitian Creole](/wiki/Haitian_Creole "Haitian Creole") (1.6%), [Vietnamese](/wiki/Vietnamese_language "Vietnamese language") (1.1%), and [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language") (1.0%).

### Immigration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Montreal&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Immigration")]

See also: [Haitians in Montreal](/wiki/Haitians_in_Montreal "Haitians in Montreal")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/Montr%C3%A9al_Population_Pyramid_%282021%29.png/250px-Montr%C3%A9al_Population_Pyramid_%282021%29.png)](/wiki/File:Montr%C3%A9al_Population_Pyramid_(2021).png)

[Population age pyramid](/wiki/Demographics_of_Canada "Demographics of Canada") of Montréal (2021 Census)

The [2021 census](/wiki/2021_Canadian_census "2021 Canadian census") reported that [immigrants](/wiki/Immigration_to_Canada "Immigration to Canada") (individuals born outside Canada) comprise 576,125 persons or 33.4% of the total population of Montreal. Of the total immigrant population, the top countries of origin were [Haiti](/wiki/Haiti "Haiti") (47,550 residents or 8.3% of the population), [Algeria](/wiki/Algeria "Algeria") (43,840; 7.6%), [France](/wiki/France "France") (39,275; 6.8%), [Morocco](/wiki/Morocco "Morocco") (33,005; 5.7%), [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy") (30,215; 5.2%), [China](/wiki/China "China") (26,335; 4.6%), the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines") (20,475; 3.6%), [Lebanon](/wiki/Lebanon "Lebanon") (17,455; 3.0%), [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam "Vietnam") (16,395; 2.8%), and [India](/wiki/India "India") (13,575; 2.4%).[[127]](#cite_note-2021censusB-135)
