The Greater Montreal Area is predominantly [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"); however, weekly church attendance in Quebec was among the lowest in Canada in 1998.[[129]](#cite_note-137) Historically, Montreal has been a centre of [Roman Catholicism](/wiki/Roman_Catholicism "Roman Catholicism") in North America with its numerous seminaries and churches, including the [Notre-Dame Basilica](/wiki/Notre-Dame_Basilica_(Montreal) "Notre-Dame Basilica (Montreal)"), the [Cathédrale Marie-Reine-du-Monde](/wiki/Cath%C3%A9drale_Marie-Reine-du-Monde "Cathédrale Marie-Reine-du-Monde"), and [Saint Joseph's Oratory](/wiki/Saint_Joseph%27s_Oratory "Saint Joseph's Oratory").

Some 49.5% of the total population was Christian in 2021,[[128]](#cite_note-Profil_Sociodémographique_Montréal_2011-136) largely Roman Catholic (35.0%), primarily because of descendants of original French settlers, and others of Italian and Irish origins. [Protestants](/wiki/Protestants "Protestants"), which include members of [Anglican](/wiki/Anglican_Church_in_Canada "Anglican Church in Canada"), [United](/wiki/United_Church_of_Canada "United Church of Canada") and [Lutheran](/wiki/Lutheran "Lutheran") churches and who are largely of British and German descent, formed 11.3% of the population; a further 3.2% consisted mostly of [Orthodox Christians](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox "Eastern Orthodox"), mainly of Greek origin but also including members of Russian, Ukrainian, and Armenian Orthodox churches.

[Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") is the largest non-Christian religious group, with 218,395 members in 2021,[[130]](#cite_note-Community_Highlights_for_Montréal-138) the second-largest concentration of Muslims in Canada at 12.7%. The [Jewish community in Montreal](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Montreal "History of the Jews in Montreal") had a population of 90,780.[[131]](#cite_note-139) In cities such as Côte Saint-Luc and Hampstead, Jewish people constitute the majority, or a substantial part of the population. In 1971 the Jewish community in Greater Montreal numbered 109,480.[[132]](#cite_note-MELS-140) Political and economic uncertainties led many to leave Montreal and the province of Quebec.[[133]](#cite_note-Am_Yisrael-141)

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Montreal&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Economy")]

Main article: [Economy of Montreal](/wiki/Economy_of_Montreal "Economy of Montreal")

Montreal has the second-largest economy of Canadian cities based on GDP[[134]](#cite_note-pricewater-142) and the largest in Quebec. In 2019, Metropolitan Montreal was responsible for CA$234.0 billion of Quebec's CA$425.3 billion GDP.[[135]](#cite_note-143) The city is today an important centre of commerce, finance, industry, technology, culture, world affairs and is the headquarters of the [Montreal Exchange](/wiki/Montreal_Exchange "Montreal Exchange"). In recent decades, the city was widely seen as weaker than that of Toronto and other major Canadian cities, but it has recently experienced a revival.[[136]](#cite_note-144)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7f/Admiralty_Chart_No_421_Montreal_Harbour%2C_Published_1961%2C_Large_Corrections_1967.jpg/250px-Admiralty_Chart_No_421_Montreal_Harbour%2C_Published_1961%2C_Large_Corrections_1967.jpg)](/wiki/File:Admiralty_Chart_No_421_Montreal_Harbour,_Published_1961,_Large_Corrections_1967.jpg)

1967 nautical chart of Montreal Harbour

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/30/Navette_Montreal_-_Ile_Charron_36.jpg/250px-Navette_Montreal_-_Ile_Charron_36.jpg)](/wiki/File:Navette_Montreal_-_Ile_Charron_36.jpg)

The [Port of Montreal](/wiki/Port_of_Montreal "Port of Montreal")

Industries include [aerospace](/wiki/Aerospace "Aerospace"), [electronic](/wiki/Electronics "Electronics") goods, [pharmaceuticals](/wiki/Pharmaceuticals "Pharmaceuticals"), printed goods, [software engineering](/wiki/Software_engineering "Software engineering"), telecommunications, textile and apparel manufacturing, tobacco, petrochemicals, and transportation. The service sector is also strong and includes [civil](/wiki/Civil_engineering "Civil engineering"), [mechanical](/wiki/Mechanical_engineering "Mechanical engineering") and [process engineering](/wiki/Process_engineering "Process engineering"), finance, higher education, and research and development. In 2002, Montreal was the fourth-largest centre in North America in terms of aerospace jobs.[[137]](#cite_note-145)
The [Port of Montreal](/wiki/Port_of_Montreal "Port of Montreal") is one of the largest [inland ports](/wiki/Inland_port "Inland port") in the world, handling 26 million [tonnes](/wiki/Tonne "Tonne") of cargo annually as of 2008.[[138]](#cite_note-PortofMontreal-146) As one of the most important ports in Canada, it remains a transshipment point for [grain](/wiki/Cereal "Cereal"), sugar, petroleum products, machinery, and consumer goods. For this reason, Montreal is the railway hub of Canada and has always been an extremely important rail city; it is home to the headquarters of the [Canadian National Railway](/wiki/Canadian_National_Railway "Canadian National Railway"),[[139]](#cite_note-CN-147) and was home to the headquarters of the [Canadian Pacific Railway](/wiki/Canadian_Pacific_Railway "Canadian Pacific Railway") until 1995.[[140]](#cite_note-CE-CP-148)

The headquarters of the [Canadian Space Agency](/wiki/Canadian_Space_Agency "Canadian Space Agency") is in Longueuil, southeast of Montreal.[[141]](#cite_note-CSA-149) Montreal also hosts the headquarters of the [International Civil Aviation Organization](/wiki/International_Civil_Aviation_Organization "International Civil Aviation Organization") (ICAO, a [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") body);[[142]](#cite_note-ICAO-150) the [World Anti-Doping Agency](/wiki/World_Anti-Doping_Agency "World Anti-Doping Agency") (an [Olympic](/wiki/International_Olympic_Committee "International Olympic Committee") body);[[143]](#cite_note-WADA-151) the [Airports Council International](/wiki/Airports_Council_International "Airports Council International") (the association of the world's airports – ACI World);[[144]](#cite_note-ACI_World-152) the [International Air Transport Association](/wiki/International_Air_Transport_Association "International Air Transport Association") (IATA),[[145]](#cite_note-IATA-153) [IATA Operational Safety Audit](/wiki/IATA_Operational_Safety_Audit "IATA Operational Safety Audit") and the International Gay and Lesbian Chamber of Commerce (IGLCC),[[146]](#cite_note-IGLCC-154) as well as some other international organizations in various fields.

Montreal is a centre of film and television production. The headquarters of [Alliance Films](/wiki/Alliance_Films "Alliance Films") and five studios of the [Academy Award](/wiki/Academy_Awards "Academy Awards")-winning [National Film Board of Canada](/wiki/National_Film_Board_of_Canada "National Film Board of Canada") are in the city, as well as the head offices of [Telefilm Canada](/wiki/Telefilm_Canada "Telefilm Canada"), the national feature-length film and television funding agency and [Télévision de Radio-Canada](/wiki/T%C3%A9l%C3%A9vision_de_Radio-Canada "Télévision de Radio-Canada"). Given its eclectic architecture and broad availability of film services and crew members, Montreal is a popular filming location for feature-length films, and sometimes stands in for European locations.[[147]](#cite_note-155)[[148]](#cite_note-Variety-156) The city is also home to many recognized cultural, film, and music festivals ([Just For Laughs](/wiki/Just_For_Laughs "Just For Laughs"), [Just For Laughs Gags](/wiki/Just_For_Laughs_Gags "Just For Laughs Gags"), [Montreal International Jazz Festival](/wiki/Montreal_International_Jazz_Festival "Montreal International Jazz Festival"), and others), which contribute significantly to its economy. It is also home to one of the world's largest cultural enterprises, the [Cirque du Soleil](/wiki/Cirque_du_Soleil "Cirque du Soleil").[[149]](#cite_note-157)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/TourDeLaBourse_night.jpg/250px-TourDeLaBourse_night.jpg)](/wiki/File:TourDeLaBourse_night.jpg)

[Tour de la Bourse](/wiki/Tour_de_la_Bourse "Tour de la Bourse") has been home to the [Montreal Exchange](/wiki/Montreal_Exchange "Montreal Exchange") from 1965 to 2018, subsequently also including offices of various companies, entities and professional firms.

Montreal is also a global hub for [artificial intelligence](/wiki/Artificial_intelligence "Artificial intelligence") research with many companies involved in this sector, such as [Facebook](/wiki/Facebook "Facebook") AI Research (FAIR), [Microsoft Research](/wiki/Microsoft_Research "Microsoft Research"), [Google Brain](/wiki/Google_Brain "Google Brain"), [DeepMind](/wiki/DeepMind "DeepMind"), [Samsung Research](/wiki/Samsung_Research "Samsung Research") and [Thales Group](/wiki/Thales_Group "Thales Group") (cortAIx).[[150]](#cite_note-158) The city is also home to [Mila (research institute)](/wiki/Mila_(research_institute) "Mila (research institute)"), an artificial intelligence research institute with over 500 researchers specializing in the field of deep learning, the largest of its kind in the world.[[151]](#cite_note-159)

The video game industry has been booming in Montreal since November 2, 1995, coinciding with the opening of [Ubisoft Montreal](/wiki/Ubisoft_Montreal "Ubisoft Montreal").[[152]](#cite_note-Develop-160) Recently, the city has attracted world leading game developers and publishers studios such as [EA](/wiki/EA_Montreal "EA Montreal"), [Eidos Interactive](/wiki/Eidos_Interactive "Eidos Interactive"), [BioWare](/wiki/BioWare "BioWare"), [Artificial Mind and Movement](/wiki/Artificial_Mind_and_Movement "Artificial Mind and Movement"), [Strategy First](/wiki/Strategy_First "Strategy First"), [THQ](/wiki/THQ "THQ"), and [Gameloft](/wiki/Gameloft "Gameloft") mainly because of the quality of local specialized labour, and tax credits offered to the corporations. In 2010, [Warner Bros. Interactive Entertainment](/wiki/Warner_Bros._Interactive_Entertainment "Warner Bros. Interactive Entertainment"), a division of [Warner Bros.](/wiki/Warner_Bros. "Warner Bros."), announced that it would open a video game studio.[[153]](#cite_note-161) Relatively new to the video game industry, it will be Warner Bros. first studio opened, not purchased, and will develop games for such Warner Bros. franchises as [Batman](/wiki/Batman "Batman") and other games from their [DC Comics](/wiki/DC_Comics "DC Comics") portfolio. The studio will create 300 jobs.

Montreal plays an important role in the finance industry. The sector employs approximately 100,000 people in the Greater Montreal Area.[[154]](#cite_note-162) As of March 2018, Montreal is ranked in the 12th position in the [Global Financial Centres Index](/wiki/Global_Financial_Centres_Index "Global Financial Centres Index"), a [ranking](/wiki/Ranking "Ranking") of the [competitiveness](/wiki/Competition_(companies) "Competition (companies)") of [financial centres](/wiki/Financial_centre "Financial centre") around the world.[[155]](#cite_note-163) The city is home to the Montreal Exchange, the oldest stock exchange in Canada and the only financial derivatives exchange in the country.[[156]](#cite_note-tourisme-montreal.org-164) The corporate headquarters of the [Bank of Montreal](/wiki/Bank_of_Montreal "Bank of Montreal") and [Royal Bank of Canada](/wiki/Royal_Bank_of_Canada "Royal Bank of Canada"), two of the biggest banks in Canada, were in Montreal. While both banks moved their headquarters to Toronto, Ontario, their legal corporate offices remain in Montreal. The city is home to head offices of two smaller banks, [National Bank of Canada](/wiki/National_Bank_of_Canada "National Bank of Canada") and [Laurentian Bank of Canada](/wiki/Laurentian_Bank_of_Canada "Laurentian Bank of Canada"). The [Caisse de dépôt et placement du Québec](/wiki/Caisse_de_d%C3%A9p%C3%B4t_et_placement_du_Qu%C3%A9bec "Caisse de dépôt et placement du Québec"), an institutional investor managing assets totalling $408 billion CAD, has its main business office in Montreal.[[157]](#cite_note-165) Many foreign subsidiaries operating in the financial sector also have offices in Montreal, including [HSBC](/wiki/HSBC "HSBC"), [Aon](/wiki/Aon_(company) "Aon (company)"), [Société Générale](/wiki/Soci%C3%A9t%C3%A9_G%C3%A9n%C3%A9rale "Société Générale"), [BNP Paribas](/wiki/BNP_Paribas "BNP Paribas") and [AXA](/wiki/AXA "AXA").[[156]](#cite_note-tourisme-montreal.org-164)[[158]](#cite_note-166)

Several companies are headquartered in Greater Montreal Area including [Rio Tinto Alcan](/wiki/Rio_Tinto_Alcan "Rio Tinto Alcan"),[[159]](#cite_note-167) [Bombardier Inc.](/wiki/Bombardier_Inc. "Bombardier Inc."),[[160]](#cite_note-168) [Canadian National Railway](/wiki/Canadian_National_Railway "Canadian National Railway"),[[161]](#cite_note-169) [CGI Group](/wiki/CGI_Inc. "CGI Inc."),[[162]](#cite_note-170) [Air Canada](/wiki/Air_Canada "Air Canada"),[[163]](#cite_note-171) [Air Transat](/wiki/Air_Transat "Air Transat"),[[164]](#cite_note-172) [CAE](/wiki/CAE_(company) "CAE (company)"),[[165]](#cite_note-173) [Saputo](/wiki/Saputo_Incorporated "Saputo Incorporated"),[[166]](#cite_note-174) [Cirque du Soleil](/wiki/Cirque_du_Soleil "Cirque du Soleil"), [Stingray Group](/wiki/Stingray_Group "Stingray Group"), [Quebecor](/wiki/Quebecor "Quebecor"),[[167]](#cite_note-175) [Ultramar](/wiki/Ultramar "Ultramar"), [Kruger Inc.](/wiki/Kruger_Inc. "Kruger Inc."), [Jean Coutu Group](/wiki/Jean_Coutu_Group "Jean Coutu Group"),[[168]](#cite_note-176) [Uniprix](/wiki/Uniprix "Uniprix"),[[169]](#cite_note-177) [Proxim](/wiki/Proxim_(pharmacy) "Proxim (pharmacy)"),[[170]](#cite_note-178) [Domtar](/wiki/Domtar "Domtar"), [Le Château](/wiki/Le_Ch%C3%A2teau "Le Château"),[[171]](#cite_note-179) [Power Corporation](/wiki/Power_Corporation "Power Corporation"), [Cellcom Communications](/wiki/Cellcom_Communications "Cellcom Communications"),[[172]](#cite_note-180) [Bell Canada](/wiki/Bell_Canada "Bell Canada").[[173]](#cite_note-181) [Standard Life](/wiki/Standard_Life_(Canada) "Standard Life (Canada)"),[[174]](#cite_note-182) [Hydro-Québec](/wiki/Hydro-Qu%C3%A9bec "Hydro-Québec"), [AbitibiBowater](/wiki/AbitibiBowater "AbitibiBowater"), [Pratt and Whitney Canada](/wiki/Pratt_and_Whitney_Canada "Pratt and Whitney Canada"), [Molson](/wiki/Molson "Molson"),[[175]](#cite_note-183) [Tembec](/wiki/Tembec "Tembec"), [Canada Steamship Lines](/wiki/Canada_Steamship_Lines "Canada Steamship Lines"), [Fednav](/wiki/Fednav "Fednav"), [Alimentation Couche-Tard](/wiki/Alimentation_Couche-Tard "Alimentation Couche-Tard"), [SNC-Lavalin](/wiki/SNC-Lavalin "SNC-Lavalin"),[[176]](#cite_note-184) [MEGA Brands](/wiki/MEGA_Brands "MEGA Brands"),[[177]](#cite_note-185) [Aeroplan](/wiki/Aeroplan "Aeroplan"),[[178]](#cite_note-186) [Agropur](/wiki/Agropur "Agropur"),[[179]](#cite_note-187) [Metro Inc.](/wiki/Metro_Inc. "Metro Inc."),[[180]](#cite_note-188) [Laurentian Bank of Canada](/wiki/Laurentian_Bank_of_Canada "Laurentian Bank of Canada"),[[181]](#cite_note-189) [National Bank of Canada](/wiki/National_Bank_of_Canada "National Bank of Canada"),[[182]](#cite_note-190) [Transat A.T.](/wiki/Transat_A.T. "Transat A.T."),[[183]](#cite_note-191) [Via Rail](/wiki/Via_Rail "Via Rail"),[[184]](#cite_note-192) [GardaWorld](/wiki/GardaWorld "GardaWorld"), [Novacam Technologies](/wiki/Novacam_Technologies "Novacam Technologies"), SOLABS,[[185]](#cite_note-193) [Dollarama](/wiki/Dollarama "Dollarama"),[[186]](#cite_note-194) [Rona](/wiki/Rona_(company) "Rona (company)"),[[187]](#cite_note-195) Lallemand[[188]](#cite_note-196) and the [Caisse de dépôt et placement du Québec](/wiki/Caisse_de_d%C3%A9p%C3%B4t_et_placement_du_Qu%C3%A9bec "Caisse de dépôt et placement du Québec").

The [Montreal Oil Refining Centre](/wiki/Montreal_Oil_Refining_Centre "Montreal Oil Refining Centre") is the largest refining centre in Canada, with companies like [Petro-Canada](/wiki/Petro-Canada "Petro-Canada"), [Ultramar](/wiki/Ultramar "Ultramar"), [Gulf Oil](/wiki/Gulf_Oil "Gulf Oil"), Petromont, Ashland Canada, Parachem Petrochemical, Coastal Petrochemical, Interquisa ([Cepsa](/wiki/Cepsa "Cepsa")) Petrochemical, [Nova Chemicals](/wiki/Nova_Chemicals "Nova Chemicals"), and more. Shell decided to close the refining centre in 2010, throwing hundreds out of work and causing an increased dependence on foreign refineries for eastern Canada.

## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Montreal&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Culture")]

Main article: [Culture of Montreal](/wiki/Culture_of_Montreal "Culture of Montreal")

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This section **needs additional citations for [verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this article](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Montreal "Special:EditPage/Montreal") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners") in this section. Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *(May 2022)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

Montreal was referred to as "Canada's Cultural Capital" by [*Monocle* magazine](/wiki/Monocle_(2007_magazine) "Monocle (2007 magazine)").[[26]](#cite_note-Monocle-28) The city is Canada's centre for French-language television productions, radio, theatre, film, multimedia, and print publishing. Montreal's many cultural communities have given it a distinct local culture. Montreal was designated as the [World Book Capital](/wiki/World_Book_Capital "World Book Capital") for the year 2005 by [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO").[[189]](#cite_note-197)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/29/Montreal_Clock_Tower%2C_Fireworks.jpg/250px-Montreal_Clock_Tower%2C_Fireworks.jpg)](/wiki/File:Montreal_Clock_Tower,_Fireworks.jpg)

[Montreal Fireworks Festival](/wiki/Montreal_Fireworks_Festival "Montreal Fireworks Festival") is the world's largest annual fireworks festival. The city hosts a number of festivals annually.

Being at the confluence of the French and English traditions, Montreal has developed a unique and distinguished cultural face. The city has produced much talent in the fields of visual arts, theatre, dance, and music, with a tradition of producing both jazz and rock music. Another distinctive characteristic of cultural life is the vibrancy of its downtown, particularly during summer, prompted by cultural and social events, including its [more than 100 annual festivals](/wiki/List_of_festivals_and_parades_in_Montreal "List of festivals and parades in Montreal"), the largest being the [Montreal International Jazz Festival](/wiki/Montreal_International_Jazz_Festival "Montreal International Jazz Festival") which is the largest jazz festival in the world. Other popular events have included [Just for Laughs](/wiki/Just_for_Laughs "Just for Laughs") (the largest comedy festival in the world), the [Montreal World Film Festival](/wiki/Montreal_World_Film_Festival "Montreal World Film Festival"), the [Festival du nouveau cinéma](/wiki/Festival_du_nouveau_cin%C3%A9ma "Festival du nouveau cinéma"), the [Fantasia Film Festival](/wiki/Fantasia_Film_Festival "Fantasia Film Festival"), [Les FrancoFolies de Montréal](/wiki/Les_FrancoFolies_de_Montr%C3%A9al "Les FrancoFolies de Montréal"), [Nuits d'Afrique](/w/index.php?title=Nuits_d%27Afrique&action=edit&redlink=1 "Nuits d'Afrique (page does not exist)")[[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Festival_international_Nuits_d%27Afrique "fr:Festival international Nuits d'Afrique")], [Pop Montreal](/wiki/Pop_Montreal "Pop Montreal"), [Divers/Cité](/wiki/Divers/Cit%C3%A9 "Divers/Cité"), [Fierté Montréal](/wiki/Fiert%C3%A9_Montr%C3%A9al "Fierté Montréal") and the [Montreal Fireworks Festival](/wiki/Montreal_Fireworks_Festival "Montreal Fireworks Festival"), [Igloofest](/wiki/Igloofest "Igloofest"), [Piknic Électronik](/wiki/Piknic_%C3%89lectronik "Piknic Électronik"), [Montréal en Lumiere](/w/index.php?title=Montr%C3%A9al_en_Lumiere&action=edit&redlink=1 "Montréal en Lumiere (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Montr%C3%A9al_en_Lumiere "fr:Montréal en Lumiere")], [Osheaga](/wiki/Osheaga_Festival "Osheaga Festival"), [Heavy Montréal](/wiki/Heavy_Montr%C3%A9al "Heavy Montréal"), Mode + Design, [Montréal complètement cirque](/w/index.php?title=Montr%C3%A9al_compl%C3%A8tement_cirque&action=edit&redlink=1 "Montréal complètement cirque (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Montr%C3%A9al_compl%C3%A8tement_cirque "fr:Montréal complètement cirque")], [MUTEK](/wiki/MUTEK "MUTEK"), [Black and Blue](/wiki/Black_and_Blue_Festival "Black and Blue Festival"), and many smaller festivals. Montreal is also widely recognized for its diverse and vibrant night life, which is considered a vital part of the local cultural ecosystem.

A cultural heart of classical art and the venue for many summer festivals, the [Place des Arts](/wiki/Place_des_Arts "Place des Arts") is a complex of different concert and theatre halls surrounding a large square in the eastern portion of downtown. Place des Arts has the headquarters of one of the world's foremost orchestras, the [Montreal Symphony Orchestra](/wiki/Montreal_Symphony_Orchestra "Montreal Symphony Orchestra"). The [Orchestre Métropolitain](/wiki/Orchestre_M%C3%A9tropolitain "Orchestre Métropolitain") and the chamber orchestra [I Musici de Montréal](/wiki/I_Musici_de_Montr%C3%A9al "I Musici de Montréal") are two other well-regarded Montreal orchestras. Also performing at Place des Arts are the [Opéra de Montréal](/wiki/Op%C3%A9ra_de_Montr%C3%A9al "Opéra de Montréal") and the city's chief ballet company [Les Grands Ballets Canadiens](/wiki/Les_Grands_Ballets_Canadiens "Les Grands Ballets Canadiens"). Internationally recognized avant-garde dance troupes such as [Compagnie Marie Chouinard](/w/index.php?title=Compagnie_Marie_Chouinard&action=edit&redlink=1 "Compagnie Marie Chouinard (page does not exist)")[[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Compagnie_Marie_Chouinard "fr:Compagnie Marie Chouinard")], [La La La Human Steps](/wiki/La_La_La_Human_Steps "La La La Human Steps"), [O Vertigo](/w/index.php?title=O_Vertigo&action=edit&redlink=1 "O Vertigo (page does not exist)")[[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/O_Vertigo "fr:O Vertigo")], and the [Fondation Jean-Pierre Perreault](/wiki/Jean-Pierre_Perreault "Jean-Pierre Perreault") have toured the world and worked with international popular artists on videos and concerts. The unique choreography of these troupes has paved the way for the success of the world-renowned Cirque du Soleil.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7b/Bas%C3%ADlica_de_Notre-Dame%2C_Montreal%2C_Canad%C3%A1%2C_2017-08-11%2C_DD_26-28_HDR.jpg/250px-Bas%C3%ADlica_de_Notre-Dame%2C_Montreal%2C_Canad%C3%A1%2C_2017-08-11%2C_DD_26-28_HDR.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bas%C3%ADlica_de_Notre-Dame,_Montreal,_Canad%C3%A1,_2017-08-11,_DD_26-28_HDR.jpg)

View of the [Notre-Dame Basilica](/wiki/Notre-Dame_Basilica_(Montreal) "Notre-Dame Basilica (Montreal)") from [Place d'Armes](/wiki/Place_d%27Armes "Place d'Armes"). The number of churches in Montreal led it to be called "the city of a hundred steeples".

Nicknamed *la ville aux cent clochers* (the city of a hundred steeples), Montreal is renowned for its churches. There are an estimated 650 churches on the island, with 450 of them dating back to the 1800s or earlier.[[190]](#cite_note-198) [Mark Twain](/wiki/Mark_Twain "Mark Twain") noted, "This is the first time I was ever in a city where you couldn't throw a brick without breaking a church window."[[191]](#cite_note-199) The city has four Roman Catholic [basilicas](/wiki/Basilica "Basilica"): [Mary, Queen of the World Cathedral](/wiki/Mary,_Queen_of_the_World_Cathedral "Mary, Queen of the World Cathedral"), [Notre-Dame Basilica](/wiki/Notre-Dame_Basilica_(Montreal) "Notre-Dame Basilica (Montreal)"), [St Patrick's Basilica](/wiki/St._Patrick%27s_Basilica,_Montreal "St. Patrick's Basilica, Montreal"), and [Saint Joseph's Oratory](/wiki/Saint_Joseph%27s_Oratory "Saint Joseph's Oratory"). The Oratory is the largest church in Canada, with the second largest copper dome in the world, after [Saint Peter's Basilica](/wiki/Saint_Peter%27s_Basilica "Saint Peter's Basilica") in Rome.[[192]](#cite_note-200)

Beginning in the 1940s, Quebec literature began to shift from pastoral tales romanticizing the [French-Canadian](/wiki/French_Canadians "French Canadians") countryside to writing set in the multicultural city of Montreal. Notable pioneering works describing the character of the city include [Gabrielle Roy](/wiki/Gabrielle_Roy "Gabrielle Roy")'s 1945 novel *[Bonheur d'occasion](/wiki/Bonheur_d%27occasion "Bonheur d'occasion")*, translated as *The Tin Flute*, and [Gwethalyn Graham](/wiki/Gwethalyn_Graham "Gwethalyn Graham")'s 1944 novel *[Earth and High Heaven](/wiki/Earth_and_High_Heaven "Earth and High Heaven")*. Subsequent writers of fiction who have set their work in Montreal have included [Mordecai Richler](/wiki/Mordecai_Richler "Mordecai Richler"), [Claude Jasmin](/wiki/Claude_Jasmin "Claude Jasmin"), [Michel Tremblay](/wiki/Michel_Tremblay "Michel Tremblay"), [Francine Noel](/wiki/Francine_Noel "Francine Noel"), and [Heather O'Neill](/wiki/Heather_O%27Neill "Heather O'Neill"), among [many others](/wiki/List_of_people_from_Montreal "List of people from Montreal").

## Sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Montreal&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Sports")]

Main article: [Sports in Montreal](/wiki/Sports_in_Montreal "Sports in Montreal")

The most popular sport is [ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey "Ice hockey"). The professional hockey team, the [Montreal Canadiens](/wiki/Montreal_Canadiens "Montreal Canadiens"), is one of the [Original Six](/wiki/Original_Six "Original Six") teams of the [National Hockey League](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League") (NHL), and has won an NHL-record 24 [Stanley Cup](/wiki/Stanley_Cup "Stanley Cup") championships. The Canadiens' most recent Stanley Cup victory came in [1993](/wiki/1993_Stanley_Cup_Finals "1993 Stanley Cup Finals"). They have major rivalries with the [Toronto Maple Leafs](/wiki/Toronto_Maple_Leafs "Toronto Maple Leafs") and [Boston Bruins](/wiki/Boston_Bruins "Boston Bruins"), both of which are also Original Six teams, and with the [Ottawa Senators](/wiki/Ottawa_Senators "Ottawa Senators"), the closest team geographically. The Canadiens have played at the [Bell Centre](/wiki/Bell_Centre "Bell Centre") since 1996. Prior to that, they played at the [Montreal Forum](/wiki/Montreal_Forum "Montreal Forum").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/80/CentreBell.jpg/250px-CentreBell.jpg)](/wiki/File:CentreBell.jpg)

Opened in 1996, the [Bell Centre](/wiki/Bell_Centre "Bell Centre") is a sports and entertainment complex and the home arena for the [Montreal Canadiens](/wiki/Montreal_Canadiens "Montreal Canadiens").

The [Montreal Alouettes](/wiki/Montreal_Alouettes "Montreal Alouettes") of the [Canadian Football League](/wiki/Canadian_Football_League "Canadian Football League") (CFL) play at [Percival Molson Memorial Stadium](/wiki/Percival_Molson_Memorial_Stadium "Percival Molson Memorial Stadium") on the campus of [McGill University](/wiki/McGill_University "McGill University") for their regular-season games. Late season and playoff games are sometimes played at the much larger, enclosed Olympic Stadium, which also hosted the [2008 Grey Cup](/wiki/2008_Grey_Cup "2008 Grey Cup"). The Alouettes have won the Grey Cup eight times, most recently in [2023](/wiki/111th_Grey_Cup "111th Grey Cup"). The Alouettes have had two periods on hiatus. During the second one, the [Montreal Machine](/wiki/Montreal_Machine "Montreal Machine") played in the [World League of American Football](/wiki/World_League_of_American_Football "World League of American Football") in 1991 and 1992. The [McGill Redbirds](/wiki/McGill_Redbirds_and_Martlets "McGill Redbirds and Martlets"), [Concordia Stingers](/wiki/Concordia_Stingers "Concordia Stingers"), and [Université de Montréal Carabins](/wiki/Universit%C3%A9_de_Montr%C3%A9al_Carabins "Université de Montréal Carabins") play in the [U Sports football](/wiki/U_Sports_football "U Sports football") league.

Montreal has a storied baseball history. The city was the home of the minor-league [Montreal Royals](/wiki/Montreal_Royals "Montreal Royals") of the [International League](/wiki/International_League "International League") until 1960. In 1946, [Jackie Robinson](/wiki/Jackie_Robinson "Jackie Robinson") broke the [Baseball colour line](/wiki/Baseball_colour_line "Baseball colour line") with the Royals in an emotionally difficult year; Robinson was forever grateful for the local fans' fervent support.[[193]](#cite_note-201) [Major League Baseball](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball") came to town in the form of the [Montreal Expos](/wiki/Montreal_Expos "Montreal Expos") in 1969. They played their games at [Jarry Park Stadium](/wiki/Jarry_Park_Stadium "Jarry Park Stadium") until moving into Olympic Stadium in 1977. After 36 years in Montreal, the team relocated to Washington, D.C., in 2005 and re-branded themselves as the [Washington Nationals](/wiki/Washington_Nationals "Washington Nationals").[[194]](#cite_note-202)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fa/2015_Canadian_GP_opening_lap.jpg/250px-2015_Canadian_GP_opening_lap.jpg)](/wiki/File:2015_Canadian_GP_opening_lap.jpg)

Montreal is the site of the [Canadian Grand Prix](/wiki/Canadian_Grand_Prix "Canadian Grand Prix"), an annual [Formula One](/wiki/Formula_One "Formula One") [auto race](/wiki/Auto_race "Auto race").

[CF Montréal](/wiki/CF_Montr%C3%A9al "CF Montréal") (formerly known as the Montreal Impact) are the city's professional soccer team. They play at a [soccer-specific stadium](/wiki/Soccer-specific_stadium "Soccer-specific stadium") called [Saputo Stadium](/wiki/Saputo_Stadium "Saputo Stadium"). They joined [Major League Soccer](/wiki/Major_League_Soccer "Major League Soccer") in 2012. The Montreal games of the [2007 FIFA U-20 World Cup](/wiki/2007_FIFA_U-20_World_Cup "2007 FIFA U-20 World Cup")[[195]](#cite_note-203) and [2014 FIFA U-20 Women's World Cup](/wiki/2014_FIFA_U-20_Women%27s_World_Cup "2014 FIFA U-20 Women's World Cup")[[196]](#cite_note-204) were held at Olympic Stadium, and the venue hosted Montreal games in the [2015 FIFA Women's World Cup](/wiki/2015_FIFA_Women%27s_World_Cup "2015 FIFA Women's World Cup").[[197]](#cite_note-205)

Montreal is the site of a high-profile [auto racing](/wiki/Auto_racing "Auto racing") event each year: the Canadian Grand Prix of Formula One (F1) racing. This race takes place on the [Circuit Gilles Villeneuve](/wiki/Circuit_Gilles_Villeneuve "Circuit Gilles Villeneuve") on Île Notre-Dame. In 2009, the race was dropped from the Formula One calendar, to the chagrin of some fans,[[198]](#cite_note-206) but the Canadian Grand Prix returned to the Formula One calendar in 2010. It was dropped from the calendar again in 2020 and 2021, due to [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic"), but racing resumed in 2022, with the [2022 Canadian Grand Prix](/wiki/2022_Canadian_Grand_Prix "2022 Canadian Grand Prix"). The Circuit Gilles Villeneuve also hosted a round of the [Champ Car World Series](/wiki/Champ_Car_World_Series "Champ Car World Series") from 2002 to 2007, and was home to the [NAPA Auto Parts 200](/wiki/NAPA_Auto_Parts_200 "NAPA Auto Parts 200"), a [NASCAR Nationwide Series](/wiki/NASCAR_Nationwide_Series "NASCAR Nationwide Series") race, and the Montréal 200, a [Grand Am](/wiki/Grand_American_Road_Racing_Association "Grand American Road Racing Association") [Rolex Sports Car Series](/wiki/Rolex_Sports_Car_Series "Rolex Sports Car Series") race.

[Uniprix Stadium](/wiki/Uniprix_Stadium "Uniprix Stadium"), built in 1993 on the site of Jarry Park, is used for the [National Bank Open](/wiki/Canadian_Open_(tennis) "Canadian Open (tennis)") (formerly known as the Rogers Cup) men's and women's tennis tournaments. The men's tournament is a [Masters 1000](/wiki/Masters_1000 "Masters 1000") event on the [ATP Tour](/wiki/ATP_Tour "ATP Tour"), and the women's tournament is a [Premier tournament](/wiki/WTA_Premier_tournaments "WTA Premier tournaments") on the [WTA Tour](/wiki/WTA_Tour "WTA Tour"). The men's and women's tournaments alternate between Montreal and Toronto every year.[[199]](#cite_note-YorkU-207)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/Le_Stade_Olympique_3.jpg/250px-Le_Stade_Olympique_3.jpg)](/wiki/File:Le_Stade_Olympique_3.jpg)

The [Olympic Stadium](/wiki/Olympic_Stadium_(Montreal) "Olympic Stadium (Montreal)") was built for the [1976 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1976_Summer_Olympics "1976 Summer Olympics") in Montreal. It is used by [MLS](/wiki/Major_League_Soccer "Major League Soccer")'s [CF Montréal](/wiki/CF_Montr%C3%A9al "CF Montréal") for select games.

Montreal was the host of the 1976 Summer Olympic Games. The stadium cost $1.5 billion;[[200]](#cite_note-olympics_cost-208) with interest that figure ballooned to nearly $3 billion, and was paid off in December 2006.[[201]](#cite_note-McGill-olympics-cost-209) Montreal also hosted the first ever [World Outgames](/wiki/World_Outgames "World Outgames") in the summer of 2006, attracting over 16,000 participants engaged in 35 sporting activities.

Montreal was the host city for the 17th unicycling world championship and convention ([UNICON](/wiki/UNICON "UNICON")) in August 2014.

## Media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Montreal&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Media")]

Main article: [Media in Montreal](/wiki/Media_in_Montreal "Media in Montreal")

Montreal is Canada's second-largest media market, and the centre of Canada's francophone media industry.

There are four [over-the-air](/wiki/Terrestrial_television "Terrestrial television") English-language television stations: [CBMT-DT](/wiki/CBMT-DT "CBMT-DT") ([CBC Television](/wiki/CBC_Television "CBC Television")), [CFCF-DT](/wiki/CFCF-DT "CFCF-DT") ([CTV](/wiki/CTV_Television_Network "CTV Television Network")), [CKMI-DT](/wiki/CKMI-DT "CKMI-DT") ([Global](/wiki/Global_Television_Network "Global Television Network")) and [CJNT-DT](/wiki/CJNT-DT "CJNT-DT") ([Citytv](/wiki/Citytv "Citytv")). There are also five over-the-air French-language television stations: [CBFT-DT](/wiki/CBFT-DT "CBFT-DT") ([Ici Radio-Canada](/wiki/Ici_Radio-Canada_T%C3%A9l%C3%A9 "Ici Radio-Canada Télé")), [CFTM-DT](/wiki/CFTM-DT "CFTM-DT") ([TVA](/wiki/TVA_(Canadian_TV_network) "TVA (Canadian TV network)")), [CFJP-DT](/wiki/CFJP-DT "CFJP-DT") ([Noovo](/wiki/Noovo "Noovo")), CIVM-DT ([Télé-Québec](/wiki/T%C3%A9l%C3%A9-Qu%C3%A9bec "Télé-Québec")), and [CFTU-DT](/wiki/CFTU-DT "CFTU-DT") (Canal Savoir).

Montreal has three daily newspapers, the English-language *[Montreal Gazette](/wiki/Montreal_Gazette "Montreal Gazette")* and the French-language *[Le Journal de Montréal](/wiki/Le_Journal_de_Montr%C3%A9al "Le Journal de Montréal")*, and *[Le Devoir](/wiki/Le_Devoir "Le Devoir")*; another French-language daily, *[La Presse](/wiki/La_Presse_(Canadian_newspaper) "La Presse (Canadian newspaper)")*, became an online daily in 2018. There are two free French dailies, *[Métro](/wiki/Metro_International "Metro International")* and *[24 Heures](/wiki/24_Hours_(newspaper) "24 Hours (newspaper)")*. Montreal has numerous weekly tabloids and community newspapers serving various neighbourhoods, ethnic groups and schools.

## Government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Montreal&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Government")]

Main articles: [Montreal City Council](/wiki/Montreal_City_Council "Montreal City Council") and [Mayor of Montreal](/wiki/Mayor_of_Montreal "Mayor of Montreal")

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This section **needs additional citations for [verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this article](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Montreal "Special:EditPage/Montreal") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners") in this section. Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *(May 2022)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

The head of the [city government in Montreal](/wiki/City_government_in_Montreal "City government in Montreal") is [the mayor](/wiki/Mayor_of_Montreal "Mayor of Montreal"), who is [first among equals](/wiki/First_among_equals "First among equals") in [the city council](/wiki/Montreal_City_Council "Montreal City Council"). As of 2026[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Montreal&action=edit), the mayor is [Soraya Martinez Ferrada](/wiki/Soraya_Martinez_Ferrada "Soraya Martinez Ferrada").[[202]](#cite_note-210)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c8/Hotel_de_ville_de_Montreal_03.jpg/250px-Hotel_de_ville_de_Montreal_03.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hotel_de_ville_de_Montreal_03.jpg)

Completed in 1878, [Montreal City Hall](/wiki/Montreal_City_Hall "Montreal City Hall") is the [seat of local government](/wiki/Montreal_City_Council "Montreal City Council").

The city council is a democratically elected institution and is the final decision-making authority in the city, although much power is centralized in the executive committee. The council consists of 65 members from all boroughs.[[203]](#cite_note-councillors-211) The council has jurisdiction over many matters, including public security, agreements with other governments, subsidy programs, the [environment](/wiki/Natural_environment "Natural environment"), [urban planning](/wiki/Urban_planning "Urban planning"), and a three-year capital expenditure program. The council is required to supervise, standardize or approve certain decisions made by the borough councils.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Reporting directly to the council, the executive committee exercises decision-making powers similar to those of the cabinet in a [parliamentary system](/wiki/Parliamentary_system "Parliamentary system") and is responsible for preparing various documents including [budgets](/wiki/Budget "Budget") and [by-laws](/wiki/By-law "By-law"), submitted to the council for approval. The decision-making powers of the executive committee cover, in particular, the awarding of contracts or grants, the management of human and financial resources, supplies and buildings. It may also be assigned further powers by the city council.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Standing committees are the prime instruments for public consultation. They are responsible for the public study of pending matters and for making the appropriate recommendations to the council. They also review the annual budget forecasts for departments under their jurisdiction. A public notice of meeting is published in both French and English daily newspapers at least seven days before each meeting. All meetings include a public question period. The standing committees, of which there are seven, have terms lasting two years. In addition, the City Council may decide to create special committees at any time. Each standing committee is made up of seven to nine members, including a chairman and a vice-chairman. The members are all elected municipal officers, with the exception of a representative of the government of Quebec on the public security committee.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The city is only one component of the larger Montreal Metropolitan Community (Communauté Métropolitaine de Montréal, CMM), which is in charge of planning, coordinating, and financing economic development, public transportation, garbage collection and [waste management](/wiki/Waste_management "Waste management"), etc., across the metropolitan area. The president of the CMM is the mayor of Montreal. The CMM covers 4,360 km2 (1,680 sq mi), with 3.6 million inhabitants in 2006.[[204]](#cite_note-CMM-212)

Montreal is the seat of the [judicial district](/wiki/Judicial_districts_of_Quebec "Judicial districts of Quebec") of Montreal, which includes the city and the other communities on the island.[[205]](#cite_note-213)

The island of Montreal elects 18 [Members of Parliament](/wiki/Member_of_Parliament_(Canada) "Member of Parliament (Canada)") to the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_Canada "House of Commons of Canada") in Ottawa.[[206]](#cite_note-214)

## Policing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Montreal&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Policing")]

Main article: [Service de police de la Ville de Montréal](/wiki/Service_de_police_de_la_Ville_de_Montr%C3%A9al "Service de police de la Ville de Montréal")

Law enforcement on the island itself is provided by the *[Service de Police de la Ville de Montréal](/wiki/SPVM "SPVM"),* or the SPVM for short.

## Crime

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Montreal&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Crime")]

Since 1975, when Montreal's homicide rate peaked at around 10.3 per 100,000 people with a total of 112 murders, the overall crime rate in Montreal has declined, with a few notable exceptions, reaching a minimum in 2016 with 23 murders.[[209]](#cite_note-217)[[210]](#cite_note-montreal.ctvnews.ca-218) Sex crimes have increased 14.5 per cent between 2015 and 2016 and fraud cases have increased by 13 per cent over the same period.[[210]](#cite_note-montreal.ctvnews.ca-218) The major criminal organizations active in Montreal are the [Rizzuto crime family](/wiki/Rizzuto_crime_family "Rizzuto crime family"), [Hells Angels](/wiki/Hells_Angels "Hells Angels") and [West End Gang](/wiki/West_End_Gang "West End Gang"). However, in the 2020s, the city has seen an increase in overall crime, with a notable increase in homicides. 25 homicides were reported in 2020 which matched the number reported in 2019. The next year saw a 48% increase in murders with a total of 37 in 2021, giving the city a homicide rate of around 2.1 per 100,000 people. The Montreal Police Annual Report for 2021 showed that there were 144 shootings across the city, or an average of one shooting every 2.5 days. In comparison, there were 71 shootings recorded the year before.[[211]](#cite_note-219) 2022 saw another 10.8% increase in homicides, with a total of 41 being reported (giving a slightly higher homicide rate of 2.3 per 100,000 people), the highest number since 2007, when there were 42.[[212]](#cite_note-220)

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Montreal&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Education")]

Main article: [Education in Montreal](/wiki/Education_in_Montreal "Education in Montreal")

The [education system in Quebec](/wiki/Education_in_Quebec "Education in Quebec") is different from other systems in North America. Between high school (which ends at grade 11) and university, students must go through an additional school called [CEGEP](/wiki/CEGEP "CEGEP"). CEGEPs offer pre-university (2-years) and technical (3-years) programs. In Montreal, [seventeen CEGEPs](/wiki/List_of_CEGEPs "List of CEGEPs") offer courses in French and five in English.

French-language elementary and secondary public schools in Montreal are operated by the [Centre de services scolaire de Montréal](/wiki/Centre_de_services_scolaire_de_Montr%C3%A9al "Centre de services scolaire de Montréal") (CSSDM),[[213]](#cite_note-221) [Centre de services scolaire Marguerite-Bourgeoys](/wiki/Centre_de_services_scolaire_Marguerite-Bourgeoys "Centre de services scolaire Marguerite-Bourgeoys")[[214]](#cite_note-222) and the [Centre de services scolaire de la Pointe-de-l'Île](/wiki/Centre_de_services_scolaire_de_la_Pointe-de-l%27%C3%8Ele "Centre de services scolaire de la Pointe-de-l'Île").[[215]](#cite_note-223)

English-language elementary and secondary public schools on Montreal Island are operated by the [English Montreal School Board](/wiki/English_Montreal_School_Board "English Montreal School Board") and the [Lester B. Pearson School Board](/wiki/Lester_B._Pearson_School_Board "Lester B. Pearson School Board").[[216]](#cite_note-224)[[217]](#cite_note-225)

With four universities, ten other degree-awarding institutions, and 12 [CEGEPs](/wiki/CEGEP "CEGEP") in an 8 km (5.0 mi) radius, Montreal has the highest concentration of post-secondary students of all major cities in North America (4.38 students per 100 residents, followed by Boston at 4.37 students per 100 residents).[[218]](#cite_note-226)

### Higher education in English

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Montreal&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Higher education in English")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/Arts_Building%2C_McGill_University%2C_Aug_31_2022.jpg/250px-Arts_Building%2C_McGill_University%2C_Aug_31_2022.jpg)](/wiki/File:Arts_Building,_McGill_University,_Aug_31_2022.jpg)

Established in 1821, [McGill University](/wiki/McGill_University "McGill University") is the oldest operating university in Montreal.

* [McGill University](/wiki/McGill_University "McGill University") is one of Canada's leading post-secondary institutions and is widely regarded as a world-class institution. In 2025, McGill was ranked as the top medical-doctoral university in Canada for the 21st consecutive year by [Maclean's](/wiki/Maclean%27s "Maclean's").[[219]](#cite_note-227) It also ranks first in Canada and the 27th best university in the world by the [QS World University Rankings](/wiki/QS_World_University_Rankings "QS World University Rankings").[[220]](#cite_note-228)
* [Concordia University](/wiki/Concordia_University_(Montreal) "Concordia University (Montreal)") was created from the merger of [Sir George Williams University](/wiki/Concordia_University_(Montreal)#Sir_George_Williams_University "Concordia University (Montreal)") and [Loyola College](/wiki/Concordia_University_(Montreal)#Loyola_College "Concordia University (Montreal)") in 1974.[[221]](#cite_note-229) The university has been ranked as one of the top comprehensive universities in Canada by Macleans.[[222]](#cite_note-230)

### Higher education in French

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Montreal&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Higher education in French")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/91/Universit%C3%A9_de_Montr%C3%A9al_%28Roger-Gaudry%29.JPG/250px-Universit%C3%A9_de_Montr%C3%A9al_%28Roger-Gaudry%29.JPG)](/wiki/File:Universit%C3%A9_de_Montr%C3%A9al_(Roger-Gaudry).JPG)

[Université de Montréal](/wiki/Universit%C3%A9_de_Montr%C3%A9al "Université de Montréal") from the [Montreal Metro](/wiki/Montreal_Metro "Montreal Metro") [station](/wiki/Universit%C3%A9-de-Montr%C3%A9al_station "Université-de-Montréal station"). The institution is the largest university in the city.

* *[Université de Montréal](/wiki/Universit%C3%A9_de_Montr%C3%A9al "Université de Montréal")* (UdeM) is the second largest research university in Canada and ranked as one of the top universities in Canada. Two separate institutions are affiliated to the university: the *[École Polytechnique Montréal](/wiki/Polytechnique_Montr%C3%A9al "Polytechnique Montréal")* (School of Engineering) and *[HEC Montréal](/wiki/HEC_Montr%C3%A9al "HEC Montréal")* (School of Business). HEC Montreal was founded in 1907 and is considered one of the best business schools in Canada.[[223]](#cite_note-231)
* *[Université du Québec à Montréal](/wiki/Universit%C3%A9_du_Qu%C3%A9bec_%C3%A0_Montr%C3%A9al "Université du Québec à Montréal")* (*UQAM*) is the Montreal campus of *[Université du Québec](/wiki/Universit%C3%A9_du_Qu%C3%A9bec "Université du Québec")*. *UQAM* generally specializes in liberal-arts, although many programs related to the sciences are available.
  + The *Université du Québec* network also has three separately run schools in Montreal, notably the *[École de technologie supérieure](/wiki/%C3%89cole_de_technologie_sup%C3%A9rieure "École de technologie supérieure") (ETS)*, the *[École nationale d'administration publique](/wiki/%C3%89cole_nationale_d%27administration_publique "École nationale d'administration publique") (ÉNAP)* and the *[Institut national de la recherche scientifique](/wiki/Institut_national_de_la_recherche_scientifique "Institut national de la recherche scientifique") (INRS)*.
* *L'Institut de formation théologique de Montréal des Prêtres de Saint-Sulpice* (*IFTM*) specializes in theology and philosophy.
* *Institut d'hôtellerie et de tourisme du Québec* (*IHTQ*) offers an Applied Bachelor in Hospitality and Hotel Management.
* *[Conservatoire de musique du Québec à Montréal](/wiki/Conservatoire_de_musique_du_Qu%C3%A9bec_%C3%A0_Montr%C3%A9al "Conservatoire de musique du Québec à Montréal")* offers both a [Bachelor](/wiki/Bachelor%27s_degree "Bachelor's degree") and a [Master](/wiki/Master%27s_degree "Master's degree") program in classical music.

Additionally, two French-language universities, [Université de Sherbrooke](/wiki/Universit%C3%A9_de_Sherbrooke "Université de Sherbrooke") and [Université Laval](/wiki/Universit%C3%A9_Laval "Université Laval") have campuses in the nearby suburb of Longueuil on Montreal's [south shore](/wiki/South_Shore_(Montreal) "South Shore (Montreal)"). Also, l'*[Institut de pastorale des Dominicains](/wiki/Institut_de_pastorale_des_Dominicains "Institut de pastorale des Dominicains")* is Montreal's university centre of Ottawa's [Collège Universitaire Dominicain](/wiki/Coll%C3%A8ge_Universitaire_Dominicain "Collège Universitaire Dominicain")/[Dominican University College](/wiki/Dominican_University_College "Dominican University College"). The *Faculté de théologie évangélique* is [Nova Scotia](/wiki/Nova_Scotia "Nova Scotia")'s [Acadia University](/wiki/Acadia_University "Acadia University") Montreal based serving French Protestant community in Canada by offering both a Bachelor and a Master program in theology

## Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Montreal&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Transportation")]

Main article: [Transportation in Montreal](/wiki/Transportation_in_Montreal "Transportation in Montreal")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dd/Montreal_autoroute.jpg/250px-Montreal_autoroute.jpg)](/wiki/File:Montreal_autoroute.jpg)

Montreal is a hub for Quebec's [autoroute](/wiki/Autoroutes_of_Quebec "Autoroutes of Quebec") system of [controlled-access highways](/wiki/Controlled-access_highway "Controlled-access highway").

Like many major cities, Montreal has a problem with vehicular traffic congestion. Commuting traffic from the cities and towns in the West Island (such as [Dollard-des-Ormeaux](/wiki/Dollard-des-Ormeaux "Dollard-des-Ormeaux") and [Pointe-Claire](/wiki/Pointe-Claire "Pointe-Claire")) is compounded by commuters entering the city that use twenty-four road crossings from numerous off-island suburbs on the [North](/wiki/North_Shore_(Laval) "North Shore (Laval)") and South Shores. The width of the Saint Lawrence River has made the construction of fixed links to the south shore expensive and difficult. There are presently four road bridges (including two of the country's busiest) along with one bridge-tunnel, two railway bridges, and a metro line. The far narrower Rivière des Prairies to the city's north, separating Montreal from Laval, is spanned by nine road bridges (seven to the city of Laval and two that span directly to the north shore) and a Metro line.

The island of Montreal is a hub for the Quebec [Autoroute](/wiki/Autoroute_(Quebec) "Autoroute (Quebec)") system, and is served by Quebec Autoroutes [A-10](/wiki/Quebec_Autoroute_10 "Quebec Autoroute 10") (known as the Bonaventure Expressway on the island of Montreal), [A-15](/wiki/Quebec_Autoroute_15 "Quebec Autoroute 15") (aka the Décarie Expressway south of the A-40 and the Laurentian Autoroute to the north of it), [A-13](/wiki/Quebec_Autoroute_13 "Quebec Autoroute 13") (aka Chomedey Autoroute), [A-20](/wiki/Quebec_Autoroute_20 "Quebec Autoroute 20"), [A-25](/wiki/Quebec_Autoroute_25 "Quebec Autoroute 25"), [A-40](/wiki/Quebec_Autoroute_40 "Quebec Autoroute 40") (part of the [Trans-Canada Highway](/wiki/Trans-Canada_Highway "Trans-Canada Highway") system, and known as "The Metropolitan" or simply "The Met" in its elevated mid-town section), [A-520](/wiki/Quebec_Autoroute_520 "Quebec Autoroute 520") and [R-136](/wiki/Quebec_Route_136_(Montreal) "Quebec Route 136 (Montreal)") (aka the Ville-Marie Autoroute). Many of these Autoroutes are frequently congested at [rush hour](/wiki/Rush_hour "Rush hour").[[224]](#cite_note-epomm-232) However, in recent years, the government has acknowledged this problem and is working on long-term solutions to alleviate the congestion. One such example is the extension of [Quebec Autoroute 30](/wiki/Quebec_Autoroute_30 "Quebec Autoroute 30") on Montreal's south shore, which will be a [bypass](/wiki/Bypass_(road) "Bypass (road)") for trucks and intercity traffic.[[225]](#cite_note-TransportsQuebec-233)

### Société de transport de Montréal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Montreal&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Société de transport de Montréal")]

Main articles: [Société de transport de Montréal](/wiki/Soci%C3%A9t%C3%A9_de_transport_de_Montr%C3%A9al "Société de transport de Montréal") and [Montreal Metro](/wiki/Montreal_Metro "Montreal Metro")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/M%C3%A9tro_Acadie.jpg/250px-M%C3%A9tro_Acadie.jpg)](/wiki/File:M%C3%A9tro_Acadie.jpg)

A train departs from [Acadie station](/wiki/Acadie_station "Acadie station"). The [Montreal Metro](/wiki/Montreal_Metro "Montreal Metro") has [68 stations](/wiki/List_of_Montreal_Metro_stations "List of Montreal Metro stations") and four lines.

Public local transport is served by a network of buses, subways, and commuter trains that extend across and off the island. The subway and bus system are operated by the [Société de transport de Montréal](/wiki/Soci%C3%A9t%C3%A9_de_transport_de_Montr%C3%A9al "Société de transport de Montréal") (STM, 'Montreal Transit Company'). The [STM bus network](/wiki/List_of_Montreal_bus_routes "List of Montreal bus routes") consists of 203 daytime and 23 night time routes. STM bus routes serve 1,347,900 passengers on an average weekday in 2010.[[226]](#cite_note-apta.com-234) It also provides adapted transport and wheelchair-accessible buses.[[227]](#cite_note-235) The STM won the award of Outstanding Public Transit System in North America by the APTA in 2010. It was the first time a Canadian company won this prize.

The [Metro](/wiki/Montreal_Metro "Montreal Metro") was inaugurated in 1966 and has 68 stations on four lines.[[228]](#cite_note-236) Total daily passengers is 1,050,800 passengers on an average weekday (as of Q1 2010).[[226]](#cite_note-apta.com-234) Each station was designed by different architects with individual themes and features original artwork, and the trains run on rubber tires, making the system quieter than most.[[229]](#cite_note-237) The project was initiated by Montreal Mayor [Jean Drapeau](/wiki/Jean_Drapeau "Jean Drapeau"), who later brought the Summer Olympic Games to Montreal in 1976. The Metro system has long had a station on the South Shore in [Longueuil](/wiki/Longueuil,_Quebec "Longueuil, Quebec"), and in 2007 was extended to the city of Laval, north of Montreal, with three new stations.[[230]](#cite_note-238) Starting in 2019, the metro has been modernizing its trains, running new *[Azur](/wiki/MPM-10 "MPM-10")* models with inter-connected wagons.[[231]](#cite_note-239)

### Air

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Montreal&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Air")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2d/AirCanadaHQMontreal.jpg/250px-AirCanadaHQMontreal.jpg)](/wiki/File:AirCanadaHQMontreal.jpg)

An [Air Canada](/wiki/Air_Canada "Air Canada") flight flies past the company's corporate headquarters, at [Montréal–Trudeau International Airport](/wiki/Montr%C3%A9al%E2%80%93Trudeau_International_Airport "Montréal–Trudeau International Airport").

Montreal has two international airports, one for passengers only, the other for cargo. [Pierre Elliott Trudeau International Airport](/wiki/Montr%C3%A9al-Pierre_Elliott_Trudeau_International_Airport "Montréal-Pierre Elliott Trudeau International Airport") (also known as *Dorval Airport*) in the City of [Dorval](/wiki/Dorval "Dorval") serves all commercial passenger traffic and is the headquarters of Air Canada[[232]](#cite_note-Air_Canada_Centre-240) and Air Transat.[[233]](#cite_note-Air_Transat_Head_Office-241) To the north of the city is [Montreal Mirabel International Airport](/wiki/Montr%C3%A9al-Mirabel_International_Airport "Montréal-Mirabel International Airport") in [Mirabel](/wiki/Mirabel,_Quebec "Mirabel, Quebec"), which was envisioned as Montreal's primary airport but which now serves cargo flights along with [MEDEVACs](/wiki/MEDEVAC "MEDEVAC") and [general aviation](/wiki/General_aviation "General aviation") and some passenger services.[[234]](#cite_note-Last_passengers_leave_Mirabel,_November_1,_2004-242)[[235]](#cite_note-medivac-243)[[236]](#cite_note-flight1-244)[[237]](#cite_note-fleet-245)[[238]](#cite_note-246) In 2018, Trudeau was the [third busiest airport in Canada](/wiki/List_of_the_busiest_airports_in_Canada "List of the busiest airports in Canada") by passenger traffic and aircraft movements, handling 19.42 million passengers,[[239]](#cite_note-247)[[240]](#cite_note-move-248) and 240,159 aircraft movements.[[241]](#cite_note-2014move-249) With 63% of its passengers being on non-domestic flights it has the largest percentage of international flights of any Canadian airport.[[242]](#cite_note-pax-250)

It is one of Air Canada's major [hubs](/wiki/Airline_hub "Airline hub") and operates on average approximately 2,400 flights per week between Montreal and 155 destinations, spread on five [continents](/wiki/Continents "Continents").

Airlines servicing Trudeau offer year-round [non-stop flights](/wiki/Non-stop_flight "Non-stop flight") to five continents, namely Africa, Asia, Europe, North America and South America.[[243]](#cite_note-251)[[244]](#cite_note-252)[[245]](#cite_note-253) It is one of only two airports in Canada with direct flights to five continents or more.

### Rail

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Montreal&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Rail")]

Montreal-based Via Rail Canada provides rail service to other cities in Canada, particularly to Quebec City and Toronto along the [Quebec City – Windsor Corridor](/wiki/Quebec_City_%E2%80%93_Windsor_Corridor "Quebec City – Windsor Corridor"). [Amtrak](/wiki/Amtrak "Amtrak"), the U.S. national passenger rail system, operates its *[Adirondack](/wiki/Adirondack_(Amtrak) "Adirondack (Amtrak)")* daily to New York. All intercity trains and most [commuter trains](/wiki/Commuter_train "Commuter train") operate out of [Central Station](/wiki/Central_Station_(Montreal) "Central Station (Montreal)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2f/Gare_centrale_de_Montreal_-_011.jpg/250px-Gare_centrale_de_Montreal_-_011.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gare_centrale_de_Montreal_-_011.jpg)

[Central Station](/wiki/Central_Station_(Montreal) "Central Station (Montreal)") is a major [inter-city](/wiki/Inter-city_rail "Inter-city rail") and [commuter rail](/wiki/Commuter_rail "Commuter rail") hub for the city.

Canadian Pacific Railway (CPR) was founded here in 1881.[[246]](#cite_note-CPR-254) Its corporate headquarters occupied [Windsor Station](/wiki/Windsor_Station_(Montreal) "Windsor Station (Montreal)") at 910 [Peel Street](/wiki/Peel_Street,_Montreal "Peel Street, Montreal") until 1995, when it moved to [Calgary](/wiki/Calgary "Calgary"), Alberta.[[140]](#cite_note-CE-CP-148) With the Port of Montreal kept open year-round by icebreakers, lines to Eastern Canada became surplus, and now Montreal is the eastern and intermodal freight terminus of CPR's successor company, [Canadian Pacific Kansas City](/wiki/Canadian_Pacific_Kansas_City "Canadian Pacific Kansas City") (CPKC).[[247]](#cite_note-CP-Map-255) CPKC connects at Montreal with the Port of Montreal, the [Delaware and Hudson Railway](/wiki/Delaware_and_Hudson_Railway "Delaware and Hudson Railway") to New York, the [Quebec Gatineau Railway](/wiki/Quebec_Gatineau_Railway "Quebec Gatineau Railway") to Quebec City and [Buckingham](/wiki/Buckingham,_Quebec "Buckingham, Quebec"), the [Central Maine and Quebec Railway](/wiki/Central_Maine_and_Quebec_Railway "Central Maine and Quebec Railway") to Halifax, and [Canadian National Railway](/wiki/Canadian_National_Railway "Canadian National Railway") (CN). The CPR's flagship train, *[The Canadian](/wiki/The_Canadian "The Canadian")*, ran daily from Windsor Station to [Vancouver](/wiki/Vancouver "Vancouver"), but in 1978 all passenger services were transferred to Via. Since 1990, *The Canadian* has terminated in Toronto instead of in Montreal.

Montreal-based CN was formed in 1919 by the Canadian government following a series of country-wide rail bankruptcies. It was formed from the [Grand Trunk](/wiki/Grand_Trunk_Railway "Grand Trunk Railway"), Midland and [Canadian Northern Railways](/wiki/Canadian_Northern_Railway "Canadian Northern Railway"), and has risen to become CPR's chief rival in freight carriage in Canada.[[248]](#cite_note-CN-History-256) Like the CPR, CN divested itself of passenger services in favour of Via.[[249]](#cite_note-CN-Hist2-257) CN's flagship train, the *[Super Continental](/wiki/Super_Continental "Super Continental")*, ran daily from Central Station to Vancouver and subsequently became a Via train in 1978. It was eliminated in 1990 in favour of rerouting *The Canadian*.

The commuter rail system is managed and operated by [Exo](/wiki/Exo_commuter_rail "Exo commuter rail"), and reaches the outlying areas of Greater Montreal with six lines. It carried an average of 79,000 daily passengers in 2014, making it the seventh busiest in North America following New York, Chicago, Toronto, Boston, Philadelphia, and Mexico City.[[250]](#cite_note-EMTA-258)

On April 22, 2016, the forthcoming automated [rapid transit](/wiki/Rapid_transit "Rapid transit") system, the [Réseau express métropolitain](/wiki/R%C3%A9seau_express_m%C3%A9tropolitain "Réseau express métropolitain") (REM), was unveiled. Groundbreaking occurred April 12, 2018, and construction of the 67-kilometre-long (42 mi) network – consisting of three branches, 26 stations, and the conversion of the region's busiest commuter railway – commenced the following month. The first branch was opened on July 31, 2023, following another extension of the current branch on November 17, 2025. Two more phases are planned to open as of 2025[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Montreal&action=edit), the REM will be fully completed by 2027,[[251]](#cite_note-259) becoming the fourth largest automated rapid transit network after the [Dubai Metro](/wiki/Dubai_Metro "Dubai Metro"), the [Singapore Mass Rapid Transit](/wiki/Mass_Rapid_Transit_(Singapore) "Mass Rapid Transit (Singapore)"), and the [Vancouver SkyTrain](/wiki/SkyTrain_(Vancouver) "SkyTrain (Vancouver)"). Most of it will be financed by pension fund manager Caisse de dépôt et placement du Québec (CDPQ Infra).[[252]](#cite_note-260)

### Bike Share Program

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Montreal&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Bike Share Program")]

Main articles: [BIXI Montréal](/wiki/BIXI_Montr%C3%A9al "BIXI Montréal") and [PBSC Urban Solutions](/wiki/PBSC_Urban_Solutions "PBSC Urban Solutions")

The city of Montreal is world-renowned for being in the top 20 most cyclist-friendly cities around the globe.[[253]](#cite_note-261) It follows that they have one of the world's most successful bike share systems in [BIXI.](/wiki/BIXI_Montr%C3%A9al "BIXI Montréal") First launched in 2009[[254]](#cite_note-262) with Montreal-based PBSC Urban Solutions ICONIC bikes, the bicycle-sharing scheme has since grown its fleet to include 750 docking and charging stations across the different neighbourhoods with 9000 bikes available for users.[[255]](#cite_note-263) In what the [STM](/wiki/Soci%C3%A9t%C3%A9_de_transport_de_Montr%C3%A9al "Société de transport de Montréal") states is a mission to combine different forms of mobility, transit card holders can now take advantage of their membership to also rent bicycles at select stations.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
